items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Modal components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the modal technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Modal implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., ModalComponentTypes) implementing ModalComponentType to declare which modal variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your ModalUiElement field registry (e.g., ModalFields) so each modal locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Modal implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define ModalComponentType enums to represent the modal technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from ModalFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Modal implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Test-side annotation that configures UI authentication for a test method. It declares which credentials provider to use (LoginCredentials), which UI login client performs the login flow (BaseLoginClient), and whether resolved credentials should be cached.

usages.description: Configure a test method to authenticate via UI before the test flow runs. The framework resolves credentials from the provided LoginCredentials implementation and delegates the login steps to the specified BaseLoginClient implementation.
usages.contextHint: Use for declarative UI authentication. The login client should perform the login steps and store resulting state (session/cookies/tokens) in browser state and/or quest storage for later use in the test.

items.summary: Specifies the credentials provider class used for UI login (must implement LoginCredentials).

usages.description: Point the annotation to a LoginCredentials implementation that supplies the login data required by the UI login client (e.g., username/password).
usages.contextHint: Project-side: implement LoginCredentials per role/persona (admin/user/etc.) if needed. Test-side: select the appropriate credentials provider for the scenario.

items.summary: Controls whether resolved credentials are cached for reuse across tests (when supported by the framework). Defaults to false when omitted.

usages.description: Enable credential caching to speed up suites when credential resolution is expensive or repeated frequently.
usages.contextHint: Defaults to false when omitted. Enable caching only when it won’t introduce unwanted shared state between tests (e.g., rotating credentials, per-test isolation requirements).

items.summary: Specifies the UI login client class that performs the login flow (must extend BaseLoginClient).

usages.description: Select the concrete UI login client used to perform authentication. The client executes the login steps using the provided UiServiceFluent and confirms success via a stable “logged-in” locator.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: implement BaseLoginClient for your application’s login flow (navigate, fill credentials, submit, confirm authenticated state). Test-side: select the login client that matches the app/variant you’re running.

items.summary: Fluent service for modal dialogs (open/close interactions, state checks, value/text retrieval and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides modal() -> ModalServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() using enum-backed ModalUiElement registries.

usages.description: Expose ModalServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the modal fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal(). Then interact with modals using enum-backed ModalUiElement constants (ModalFields.*) to validate modal state/title/body and close it, finishing with drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ModalServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and should not be instantiated manually in tests. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.modal() (delegating to getModalField()). Test-side: prefer ModalFields.* (ModalUiElement) over raw By/WebElement; validate modal state/content only when it matters to the scenario.

items.summary: Read the modal’s content title (section/header title) and store it in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .getContentTitle(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a modal content/header title for later comparison or conditional logic in the same test flow.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the value later from Quest storage. If you want an immediate assertion, prefer validateContentTitle(...).

items.summary: Validate that the modal title matches the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateTitle(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, true, "Help")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate a modal title. When soft=true the framework aggregates failures; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI aspects in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the modal is closed (not visible).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateIsClosed(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a modal is not open before continuing with interactions on the underlying page.
usages.contextHint: Use when closed state is a strict precondition. Validations are optional, use this only when the scenario depends on the modal being closed.

items.summary: Validate that the modal is closed (not visible), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateIsClosed(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate modal closed state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when checking multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Read the modal title and store it in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .getTitle(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a modal title for later comparison or conditional logic in the same test flow.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the value later from Quest storage. If you want an immediate assertion, prefer validateTitle(...).

items.summary: Close/dismiss the modal and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .close(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Close a modal via its close control to return to the underlying page flow.
usages.contextHint: Prefer modal().close(...) when the flow is “dismiss or close the modal”. If the scenario requires clicking a specific modal action button, use modal().click(...).

items.summary: Hard-assert that the modal body text matches the expected value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateBodyText(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, "Action completed")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion of the modal’s main message/feedback text.
usages.contextHint: Use when body text must match to proceed. For aggregated checks, use the soft-enabled overload with the boolean flag.

items.summary: Validate that the modal body text matches the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateBodyText(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, true, "Welcome")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate modal body text. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the modal’s content title (section/header title) matches the expected value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateContentTitle(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, "Help")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion of a sectioned modal’s content/header title.
usages.contextHint: Use when content title must match to proceed. For aggregated checks, use the soft-enabled overload with the boolean flag.

items.summary: Click the modal’s primary/action control configured by the given ModalUiElement and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .click(ModalFields.CONFIRMATION_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Trigger the primary modal action (e.g., confirm/ok/continue) as defined by your modal element configuration.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: ensure the ModalUiElement is configured to target the intended action control inside the modal. Use validations (title/body/opened) only when the scenario requires them.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the modal title matches the expected value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateTitle(ModalFields.CONFIRMATION_MODAL, "Confirm Action")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that the expected modal is displayed (by title).
usages.contextHint: Use when title must match to proceed. For aggregated checks, use the soft-enabled overload with the boolean flag.

items.summary: Validate that the modal’s content title (section/header title) matches the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateContentTitle(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL, true, "Details")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate content title. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the modal is opened (visible).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateIsOpened(ModalFields.CONFIRMATION_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a modal is displayed before interacting with it.
usages.contextHint: Use when opened state is a strict precondition. Validations are optional—use this only when the scenario depends on the modal being open.

items.summary: Read the modal body text and store it in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .getBodyText(ModalFields.INFO_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture modal body text for later comparison or conditional logic in the same test flow.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the value later from Quest storage. If you want an immediate assertion, prefer validateBodyText(...).

items.summary: Validate that the modal is opened (visible), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .validateIsOpened(ModalFields.CONFIRMATION_MODAL, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate modal open state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the modal is opened (visible) and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .modal()
  .isOpened(ModalFields.CONFIRMATION_MODAL)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe modal open state when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether the modal is present.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsOpened(...) for clearer intent.

items.summary: Framework-side annotation that binds a concrete component implementation to a logical ComponentType identifier. ROA uses this identifier to resolve the correct implementation at runtime when a project element declares a matching componentType.

usages.description: Associate a concrete implementation class with a ComponentType identifier so ROA can resolve the correct component implementation for the active UI technology/variant.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: the annotation value must match the identifier used by the corresponding project ComponentType enum (e.g., ButtonComponentTypes.BOOTSTRAP_BUTTON_TYPE) so resolution is deterministic. Test-side: tests should interact through fluent services; they don’t reference implementations directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for framework-driven insertions (typed “insertion” actions executed via the UI interactor’s insertion registry). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides insertion() -> InsertionServiceFluent) so tests can trigger reusable insertions without duplicating low-level UI steps.

usages.description: Expose InsertionServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests can enter the insertion chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).insertion(). Use it to execute framework-registered insertions (data-driven UI insert actions) and then return to the main quest via drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: InsertionServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and backed by the insertion registry; do not instantiate it manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.insertion() (delegating to getInsertionService()). Use insertions to centralize repetitive UI “fill” actions behind a single call rather than duplicating steps across tests.

items.summary: Execute a framework-registered insertion for the given data object and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .insertion()
  .insertData(myRowModel)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Trigger an insertion using a composite data object (DTO/model) that the insertion registry knows how to map onto UI controls (inputs/selects/checkboxes, etc.). This keeps repetitive “fill” logic centralized and reusable.
usages.contextHint: The insertion behavior is driven by the insertion registry/configuration (annotations/registration) rather than the test itself. Use this when you have a model-to-UI mapping already defined and you want a single call to populate the form/state.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Accordion behavior across different UI technologies/variants (expand/collapse panels, read titles/content, and query expanded/collapsed/enabled state). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the AccordionComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Accordion defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with accordion-style UI widgets. Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported accordion technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding AccordionComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Accordion implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Collapses accordion panels identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based collapse is typically used by the fluent layer when panels cannot be reliably addressed by label (e.g., repeated labels, localization), or when a stable selector exists for specific panels. The concrete Accordion implementation decides how to detect expanded state and trigger collapse for the given UI technology.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep locator-based operations deterministic (resolve the correct panel/header and collapse only when needed). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of text labels for all collapsed panels within the provided accordion container.

usages.description: This method is used to query accordion state when flows need to assert which panels are closed or to branch logic based on current state. Services typically wrap this into fluent validations and reporting.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure label extraction and collapsed detection match the accordion technology (ARIA attributes, CSS classes, etc.). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of all panel labels within the provided accordion container, regardless of expanded/collapsed state.

usages.description: Used to discover all available panels in an accordion container for coverage checks, dynamic flows, or to combine with enabled/expanded/collapsed queries. Fluent services typically expose this for assertions and diagnostics.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label extraction stable (trim/normalize) and aligned with how expand/collapse address panels by text. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Expands an accordion panel selected by Strategy within the provided container and returns the chosen panel label.

usages.description: Strategy-based expansion supports dynamic interactions when a specific label is not predetermined (e.g., expand the first available panel, last panel, or a random one). Fluent services use this to drive exploration-style checks and scenario setup.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of collapsed panel labels by resolving the accordion container via locator.

usages.description: Locator-based container resolution is useful when the fluent/service layer has a stable container selector but not an already-resolved SmartWebElement instance. The implementation delegates to the container-based variant to keep behavior consistent.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator-based overloads to the SmartWebElement-based overloads to avoid duplicate logic. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the title/label text for a specific accordion panel identified by locator.

usages.description: Used when a panel is addressed by a stable selector and the flow needs to verify or log the panel title. Fluent services may expose this as a readable assertion helper for dynamic accordion layouts.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep title extraction aligned with your accordion markup (header element, button label, ARIA attributes). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Expands one or more accordion panels by label within the provided container.

usages.description: Label-based expansion is commonly used in tests and flows where panel titles are stable and meaningful. Fluent services delegate to this method when expanding specific panels within scoped UI regions (cards/modals/sections).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: normalize label matching (trim/normalize-space) and keep resolution strictly within the container. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Expands one or more accordion panels by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based expansion is useful when the accordion scope is unambiguous on the page. Fluent services often prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple accordions are present.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global resolution only when your accordion technology makes it safe to do so (avoid matching the wrong accordion instance). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified accordion panels are enabled within the provided container.

usages.description: Enabled-state checks are used by fluent validations to confirm which panels are interactive before attempting to expand/collapse. Implementations must align with the UI technology (disabled attributes, aria-disabled, blocked headers, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define enabled semantics consistently (native disabled, aria-disabled, CSS state, etc.) and ensure checks resolve the correct header/control for the panel. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Collapses one or more accordion panels by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based collapse is useful when accordion scope is unambiguous. Fluent services often prefer container-scoped overloads to avoid matching the wrong accordion instance on complex pages.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global resolution only when safe; otherwise prefer container-scoped logic to avoid collapsing a different accordion instance. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of all panel labels by resolving the accordion container via locator.

usages.description: Locator-based discovery is used when the fluent/service layer has a stable container selector. Delegating to the SmartWebElement-based overload keeps panel enumeration consistent across APIs.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to centralize enumeration logic. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Expands accordion panels identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based expansion is used when labels are non-unique, localized, or when a selector directly targets the intended panel. Fluent services can use this variant in advanced scenarios where label matching is not reliable.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators resolve the intended panel/header element and keep expansion conditional on current state. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Collapses one or more accordion panels by label within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped collapse by label is commonly used to restore a known state between steps or to close panels before proceeding. Fluent services typically expose this to keep accordion interactions readable and consistent.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep collapse conditional (only collapse if expanded) to avoid toggling a panel into the wrong state. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified accordion panels are enabled at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global enabled-state checks are useful when accordion scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations often prefer container-scoped overloads on complex pages with multiple accordions.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global matching only when safe; otherwise keep checks container-scoped to avoid evaluating the wrong accordion instance. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Collapses an accordion panel selected by Strategy within the provided container and returns the chosen panel label.

usages.description: Strategy-based collapse is useful when the panel to close is chosen dynamically (e.g., collapse the first expanded panel). Fluent services can use this for generic cleanup flows and exploratory scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the main text/content of an accordion panel identified by locator.

usages.description: Used for content verification where the panel body text must be asserted (e.g., details, instructions, dynamic data). Fluent services may wrap this into validations and Allure reporting.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure body/content resolution matches your accordion markup (body element, ARIA region, etc.). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether accordion panels identified by one or more locators are enabled.

usages.description: Locator-based enablement checks are used when labels are not reliable (non-unique or localized) and a stable selector exists per panel. Fluent validations can delegate to this method in advanced scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep locators tied to the correct panel/header control and apply enabled semantics consistently (disabled/aria-disabled/CSS). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of expanded panel labels by resolving the accordion container via locator.

usages.description: Used to query expanded state when the accordion container is addressed by a stable selector. Delegating to the container-based overload keeps expanded detection consistent across APIs.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to centralize expanded/collapsed detection logic. Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of text labels for all expanded panels within the provided accordion container.

usages.description: This method is used to assert which panels are open or to drive branching logic based on open sections. Fluent services typically expose this as part of accordion state validations and diagnostics.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure expanded detection and label extraction match the accordion technology (ARIA attributes, CSS classes, etc.). Test-side: prefer AccordionServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retry condition abstraction: a function that produces a value and a predicate that decides success. Typically created using RetryConditionImpl and passed to FluentService.retryUntil(...).

usages.description: Define a RetryCondition by pairing a value-producing function with a success predicate (most commonly via RetryConditionImpl).
usages.contextHint: The retry mechanism repeatedly calls condition.function() with the provided service object (e.g., SmartWebDriver) and checks condition.condition() until it passes or times out.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Loader components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the loader technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Loader implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., LoaderComponentTypes) implementing LoaderComponentType to declare which loader variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your LoaderUiElement field registry (e.g., LoaderFields) so each loader locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Loader implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define LoaderComponentType enums to represent the loader technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from LoaderFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Loader implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Fluent service for loader/spinner components (waits, visibility checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides loader() -> LoaderServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).loader() using enum-backed LoaderUiElement registries.

usages.description: Expose LoaderServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the loader fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).loader(). Then stabilize flows around async UI loading by waiting for loaders to appear/disappear using enum-backed LoaderUiElement constants (LoaderFields.*).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: LoaderServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and should not be instantiated manually in tests. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.loader() (delegating to getLoaderField()). Use LoaderFields.* (LoaderUiElement) to centralize loader selectors and component typing.

items.summary: Wait up to the given timeout (seconds) for the loader to become visible.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .waitToBeShown(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER, 5)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Wait briefly for a loader to appear when an action is expected to trigger loading.
usages.contextHint: Use when you specifically need to confirm the loader shows up. For a full cycle, prefer waitToBeShownAndRemoved(...).

items.summary: Wait up to the given timeout (seconds) for the loader to be removed/hidden.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .waitToBeRemoved(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER, 20)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Wait for loading to finish before continuing with UI interactions that depend on the page being stable.
usages.contextHint: Use after triggering an async operation to avoid interacting while the UI is still updating.

items.summary: Wait for the loader to be shown within the first timeout (seconds) and then removed within the second timeout (seconds).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .waitToBeShownAndRemoved(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER, 5, 20)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Perform a full loader-cycle wait around long-running actions where you expect both appearance and disappearance of a loader.
usages.contextHint: Use when the loader should appear as part of the UX and you want a stable “start → finish” synchronization point.

items.summary: Validate that the loader is hidden, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .validateIsHidden(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate loader hidden state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the loader is visible.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .validateIsVisible(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a loader is currently shown (useful when the UI is expected to display a loader during an in-progress operation).
usages.contextHint: Use when loader visibility is a strict expectation of the scenario.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the loader is hidden.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .validateIsHidden(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that loading is finished before proceeding with UI interactions.
usages.contextHint: Use when hidden state must be true to safely continue (e.g., before clicking or reading elements).

items.summary: Evaluate whether the loader is visible and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .isVisible(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe loader visibility when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether loading is currently in progress.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsVisible(...).

items.summary: Validate that the loader is visible, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .loader()
  .validateIsVisible(LoaderFields.GLOBAL_LOADER, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate loader visibility. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Table row-model annotation that binds a TableCell field to a custom cell insertion handler. Use this when a column cannot be populated via the default “type into cell” logic (e.g., the cell requires clicking an icon/button, opening a widget, or executing a specialized interaction) during table insertion/edit flows.

usages.description: Annotate a TableCell field inside a @TableInfo row model to override how ROA inserts data into that column. Instead of default insertion, ROA instantiates the provided CellInsertionFunction and calls it with the resolved cell element (and any values passed by the table insertion flow). This enables non-standard cell interactions like clicking a control, expanding a dropdown, or triggering a modal from within a table cell.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: place @CustomCellInsertion on the specific TableCell field that needs special handling and point insertionFunction() to a CellInsertionFunction implementation (often as a private static inner class near the field). Use alongside @TableCellLocator to define how the framework finds the cell/header for that column. Test-side: this is exercised through TableServiceFluent insert/edit flows; tests shouldn’t call the insertion function directly.

items.summary: Execution order for a custom cell insertion when multiple @CustomCellInsertion handlers exist for the same row model. If omitted, the order defaults to 0.

usages.description: Use order() to control sequencing when multiple custom insertions must happen in a predictable order (e.g., click an icon first, then confirm/fill a follow-up cell interaction). If you don’t specify order, it is treated as 0, which is sufficient for most single-handler cases.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: process custom insertions by their order value so multi-step row insertions remain deterministic. Project-side: set explicit order only when sequencing matters; otherwise omit it and rely on the default (0). Test-side: ordering is applied automatically during TableServiceFluent insert/edit operations.

items.summary: Defines the CellInsertionFunction class that performs the custom insertion logic for the annotated TableCell field.

usages.description: Set insertionFunction() to a class implementing CellInsertionFunction. During table insertion/edit flows, ROA resolves the target cell element for the annotated field and delegates the interaction to the provided handler, passing the cell element and any values from the insertion call. This enables custom behaviors when the cell is not a plain text input.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep the insertion function close to the TableCell definition (often as a private static inner class) and implement only the behavior needed for that specific column. Framework-side: instantiate the handler and invoke it with the resolved cell element and the provided values. Test-side: this runs as part of TableServiceFluent insert/edit flows—tests provide values via the fluent API, not by calling the handler directly.

items.summary: Per-Quest (per-test) in-memory storage. Values are stored under enum keys, optionally grouped in sub-storages (namespaces like UI/PRE_ARGUMENTS/STATIC_DATA). Most tests should NOT access Storage directly—prefer BaseQuest.retrieve(...) and extractor helpers (e.g., DataExtractorsTest/DataExtractorsUi). Direct Storage access is mainly for project-side utilities (data creators, preconditions, hooks, custom rings).

usages.description: Project-side: store values explicitly (commonly inside journeys/preconditions or helper services). This enables later retrieval from the quest flow without passing objects through every method call.
usages.contextHint: Use in project utilities (DataCreator/Preconditions/hooks/custom rings). In tests, prefer BaseQuest.retrieve(...) instead of touching Storage directly.

usages.description: Project-side: read the latest stored value for a key from a namespace sub-storage in a typed way.
usages.contextHint: Storage.getByClass(key, Class) (or equivalent typed getters) returns the latest value stored under that key. If nothing exists, it returns null.

usages.description: Read older entries when the same key was stored multiple times (useful for comparisons like before/after values in a single test flow).
usages.contextHint: Index is 1-based from the end (1 = most recent). In tests, prefer retrieve(extractor, index, type) when an extractor helper exists (e.g., DataExtractorsUi.responseBodyExtraction(..., index)).

usages.description: Access hook-scoped data stored under the HOOKS map (used by hookData(...) and Storage.getHookData(...)).
usages.contextHint: If you’re in a test class extending BaseQuest, prefer hookData(UiHookKeys.SESSION_ID, Type.class) instead of accessing the HOOKS map directly.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring alert elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s AlertService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., AlertFields) so UI assertions/actions can target strongly-typed alerts instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements AlertUiElement to centralize alert locators and map each alert to a concrete AlertComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific alert needs extra stabilization (wait for presence/visibility) or when you want a post-action condition (e.g., wait for the alert to disappear).
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep alert definitions in a single enum (e.g., AlertFields) and map each constant to the correct AlertComponentType so the framework selects the right alert component implementation. Use 2 args for stable alerts, 3 args when you need a pre-wait hook, and 4 args when you also need an after() hook (e.g., wait for toast/alert to be removed). Test-side usage is via fluent AlertService methods (validateText/validateIsVisible/etc.), which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Toggle components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the toggle technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Toggle implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., ToggleComponentTypes) implementing ToggleComponentType to declare which toggle variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your ToggleUiElement field registry (e.g., ToggleFields) so each toggle locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Toggle implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define ToggleComponentType enums to represent the toggle technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from ToggleFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Toggle implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Functional table-field descriptor used by ROA table reads/writes to map UI cells into row-model properties. A TableField wraps a “setter-style” operation (via invoke) so the table layer can project specific columns into strongly-typed row representations (instead of returning raw strings/maps).

usages.description: Use TableField to declare column-to-model bindings when reading tables with projected fields. The table layer resolves the corresponding cell values and invokes the mapped setters on your row representation class, producing a strongly-typed list of rows with only the requested fields populated.
usages.contextHint: Project/test-side: pass TableField.of(RowType::setX) bindings to TableServiceFluent read operations when you want partial projections (only specific columns). Framework-side: the table mapper calls invoke(instance, value) for each projected field; implementations should keep value conversion/normalization consistent with the row model’s expected types.

items.summary: Factory for creating a TableField from a setter-like BiConsumer (typically a method reference such as RowType::setField).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
     .table().readTable(
         Tables.OUTFLOW,
         3, 5,
         TableField.of(PaymentsTableRow::setCategory),
         TableField.of(PaymentsTableRow::setAmount)
     )
     .drop().complete();
usages.description: Create TableField bindings using method references to your row model setters. ROA uses these bindings during table reads to populate your row representation instances by invoking the provided setter for each resolved cell value.
usages.contextHint: Project/test-side: prefer method references (RowType::setX) for clear, type-safe column mappings. Framework-side: of(...) wraps the BiConsumer into a TableField implementation used by the table mapper to invoke setters during read/projection flows.

items.summary: Parameter annotation for injecting test-data models into @Test method arguments by resolving a project-defined model key (e.g., "PURCHASE_CURRENCY"). If the parameter type is a concrete model, ROA injects it eagerly; if the parameter type is Late<T>, ROA injects a lazy creator and you call create() when you’re ready.

usages.description: Eager injection: the framework resolves the model key and creates the object up-front, then injects it directly as the parameter.
usages.contextHint: Use on @Test method parameters only. model must point to a project DataForge key (e.g., "PURCHASE_CURRENCY"). Prefer this when the test data can be created before the flow starts.

usages.description: Lazy injection: inject Late<T> and call create() at the point in the flow where you need the object (useful when the model depends on runtime state or you want to delay creation until after navigation/login/preconditions).
usages.contextHint: Use when crafted data should be created later in the scenario (e.g., after the UI reaches a specific state). The model key is still the same (e.g., "PURCHASE_CURRENCY"); the framework injects Late<T> and you call create() when appropriate.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring intercept targets for UI-driven network interception. Implement this as an enum where each constant provides a URL substring to match intercepted requests and exposes its enum identity via enumImpl().

usages.description: Define an enum that implements DataIntercept to standardize interception targets. Each constant carries the URL substring that uniquely identifies the request(s) you want to capture during UI tests.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep endpoint substrings specific enough to avoid false positives and stable enough to survive minor URL changes. Tests typically reference getEndpointSubString() when validating captured traffic (status, payload extraction) via the interceptor fluent service.

items.summary: Returns the URL substring used to match which requests should be intercepted.

usages.description: Use the endpoint substring to tell the interceptor fluent service which captured request/response series to validate or extract from.
usages.contextHint: Return value should be specific enough to avoid false positives (e.g., "/login" instead of "/"). Prefer using enum-defined targets rather than hardcoding substrings throughout tests.

items.summary: Returns the enum constant that represents this intercept target (used as a stable identity key by framework routing/storage).

usages.code: Enum<?> key = RequestsInterceptor.INTERCEPT_REQUEST_AUTH.enumImpl();
usages.description: Expose the enum identity for consistent routing and storage keying when the framework needs a stable identifier for an intercept target.
usages.contextHint: Primarily used by framework glue (routing/storage/logging). Tests typically use getEndpointSubString() for validation and extraction operations.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring toggle elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s ToggleService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., ToggleFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed toggles instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements ToggleUiElement to centralize toggle locators and map each toggle to a concrete ToggleComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific toggle needs extra stabilization (wait for presence/visibility) before selecting/deselecting or validating state.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep toggle definitions in a single enum (e.g., ToggleFields) and map each constant to the correct ToggleComponentType so the framework selects the right toggle implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic toggles; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent ToggleService methods (select/deselect/validate/etc.), which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Optional container annotation for grouping multiple @Journey annotations on a single @Test method. Prefer stacking multiple @Journey annotations directly unless you specifically want the container style.

usages.description: Container style: group multiple journeys into a single annotation. Ordering is still controlled by Journey.order(). In real projects, Journey.value is typically written as a constant from an enum implementing PreQuestJourney (e.g., Preconditions implements PreQuestJourney<Preconditions>), and JourneyData values are typically written as constants from an enum implementing DataForge (e.g., DataCreator implements DataForge<DataCreator>).
usages.contextHint: Optional. In modern usage you can simply stack multiple @Journey annotations directly on the test method instead of using @PreQuest. If you use @PreQuest, keep explicit order values on each Journey so execution is deterministic.

items.summary: Framework contract for extracting a typed value from ROA storage. A DataExtractor encapsulates where to read from (namespace/key) and how to transform the stored raw object into the desired type, so tests can call retrieve(extractor, Type.class) without knowing storage internals.

usages.description: Implement DataExtractor to centralize extraction logic (read + transform) for values stored during UI flows (e.g., intercepted payload values, computed UI totals, static test data entries). This keeps tests clean and avoids repeating storage/normalization code.
usages.contextHint: Prefer reusing existing extractor utilities/registries when available. For UI-focused extractors, use DataExtractorUI helpers (if provided by your project/framework) instead of creating ad-hoc extractors throughout tests.

items.summary: JUnit test-class annotation that boots ROA UI testing by applying the required JUnit 5 extensions (e.g., UiTestExtension) and enabling adapter package scanning. This wires the UI test runtime (driver/session, Quest/storage integration, reporting hooks) so UI fluent services and annotations like @AuthenticateViaUi / @InterceptRequests work for the class.

usages.description: Annotate a test class with @UI to activate the ROA UI test runtime for all tests in the class (extensions + adapter scanning).
usages.contextHint: Use on UI test classes. Combine with @AuthenticateViaUi on test methods when login is required, and with @InterceptRequests when you need request/response interception and validation.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring input elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s InputService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., InputFields) so fluent UI actions can insert/clear/read/validate values using strongly-typed elements instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements InputUiElement to centralize input locators and map each field to a concrete InputComponentType (e.g., Bootstrap). The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific input needs extra stabilization (wait for presence/visibility) before inserting, clearing, reading, or validating values.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep input definitions in a single enum (e.g., InputFields) and map each constant to the correct InputComponentType so the framework selects the right input implementation. Use 2 args for stable fields and 3 args when you need a pre-wait hook. Test-side usage is via fluent InputService methods (insert/clear/getValue/validate/etc.), which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Base class for project-defined Rings (custom fluent services). Extend it to build reusable flows and to call other rings via quest.use(...).

usages.description: Create a project-side ring by extending FluentService (directly or via a project facade like UiServiceFluent). Ring methods should return the ring type to keep fluent chaining, and can orchestrate other rings via quest.use(...).
usages.contextHint: Project-side change. Rings are Spring beans annotated with @Ring(...), and are activated from tests via quest.use(RING_OF_UI) (or your project ring constant).

items.summary: Return from the current Ring back to Quest so you can switch rings or finish the flow.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
     .input().insert(LoginFields.USERNAME, "demo")
     .drop()
     .use(RING_OF_UI).button().click(LoginButtons.SIGN_IN)
     .drop()
     .complete();
usages.description: Switch rings (or ring sub-services) mid-flow: exit the current fluent context and return to the Quest chain.
usages.contextHint: Use when one fluent segment finishes and the next segment continues the flow. Always finish the overall flow with complete() on the quest chain.

items.summary: Ring hook invoked after the framework attaches the active SuperQuest to the ring. Override it for ring-specific initialization that depends on quest being available (storage namespaces, defaults, etc.).

usages.description: Override when the ring needs one-time initialization that depends on quest being available (e.g., creating a sub-storage namespace or setting defaults).
usages.contextHint: Project-side ring authoring. Do not call this manually in tests; it’s part of ring initialization/wiring. If your project constructs a facade manually (like AppUiService), call postQuestSetupInitialization() after assigning quest, as shown.

items.summary: Protected retry primitive used by rings. Wrap it in your ring when you want polling behavior (e.g., wait for a UI condition) without putting sleeps in tests.

usages.description: Expose retry behavior from a custom ring by wrapping the protected retryUntil(...) method and passing the correct service object into the condition.
usages.contextHint: Project-side ring authoring. The 'service' parameter must match what condition.function() expects (for UI flows this is often SmartWebDriver or a component/service instance). Use this to stabilize polling-based waits inside rings rather than in tests.

items.summary: Project-side table descriptor implemented as an enum to bind a concrete UI table to its row model (via rowsRepresentationClass), optional TableComponentType, and optional before/after hooks. TableServiceFluent uses TableElement to locate tables and perform typed table operations.

usages.description: Define a project enum implementing TableElement to register tables in a single place and bind each table to a row representation class. Tests pass these enum constants into TableServiceFluent (e.g., readTable/readRow/filterTable/sortTable) to perform typed table interactions without duplicating locators in test code.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep one Tables enum (or a few grouped enums) that represent your app’s tables and map each to its row model class annotated with @TableInfo. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() and pass the TableElement constant (e.g., Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS) rather than working with raw selectors.

items.summary: Global holder for UiConfig, providing a lazily initialized configuration instance used by UI rings/services (e.g., baseUrl, timeouts, browser settings). UiConfig is typically accessed via getUiConfig() (often statically imported) rather than constructed manually.

usages.description: Access UiConfig via UiConfigHolder.getUiConfig() (commonly used as getUiConfig()) to retrieve configuration values such as baseUrl for navigation and other UI runtime settings.
usages.contextHint: Test-side: prefer getUiConfig() instead of instantiating UiConfig directly. Framework-side: UiConfig is typically initialized via the configured cache/provider and then reused.

items.summary: Returns the global UiConfig instance, lazily creating/caching it via the configured config cache/provider when first accessed.

usages.description: Retrieve UiConfig and use its properties in UI flows (e.g., navigate to the configured base URL).
usages.contextHint: Prefer calling getUiConfig() from tests/rings rather than wiring configuration manually. If UiConfig appears null/misconfigured, verify the configured cache/provider initialization in the UI test runtime.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring modal elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s ModalService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., ModalFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed modals instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements ModalUiElement to centralize modal locators and map each modal to a concrete ModalComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific modal needs extra stabilization (wait for presence/visibility) before validating or closing it.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep modal definitions in a single enum (e.g., ModalFields) and map each constant to the correct ModalComponentType so the framework selects the right modal component implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic modals; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent ModalService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Fluent service for checkbox controls (select/deselect, read state, bulk reads, enablement checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides checkbox() -> CheckboxServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() using enum-backed CheckboxUiElement registries.

usages.description: Expose CheckboxServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the checkbox fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox(). Then interact with checkboxes using enum-backed CheckboxUiElement constants (CheckboxFields.*), finishing with drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: CheckboxServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and should not be instantiated manually in tests. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.checkbox() (delegating to getCheckboxField()). Test-side: prefer CheckboxFields.* (CheckboxUiElement) over raw By/WebElement.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the checkbox is enabled and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .isEnabled(CheckboxFields.TERMS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe enablement when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether the checkbox is interactable.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsEnabled(...).

items.summary: Hard-assert that the checkbox is enabled (interactable).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .validateIsEnabled(CheckboxFields.TERMS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that the checkbox can be interacted with.
usages.contextHint: Use when enablement is a strict precondition for the scenario.

items.summary: Deselect/uncheck the given checkbox and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .deSelect(CheckboxFields.SUBSCRIBE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Uncheck a checkbox (useful for resetting state or ensuring an opt-in is off).
usages.contextHint: Prefer deSelect(...) over direct WebElement clicks so the framework applies its checkbox component strategy and hooks.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the checkbox is selected (checked).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .validateIsSelected(CheckboxFields.TERMS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a checkbox is currently checked.
usages.contextHint: Use when selected state must be true to proceed.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the checkbox group represented by the element is enabled and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .areEnabled(CheckboxFields.NOTIFICATION_OPTIONS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe enablement across a checkbox group/container when your CheckboxUiElement represents a collection of checkboxes.
usages.contextHint: Use when your project models a checkbox group as a single CheckboxUiElement (e.g., container locator). For direct single-checkbox checks, use isEnabled(...)/validateIsEnabled(...).

items.summary: Low-level insertion entrypoint that executes a checkbox insertion using a raw locator + explicit ComponentType, passing optional insertion arguments.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .insertion(CheckboxComponentTypes.DEFAULT_CHECKBOX_TYPE, By.cssSelector("#terms"), new Object[]{ true })
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use when implementing or extending component infrastructure where you intentionally operate on dynamic locators or metadata-driven controls, but still want the framework’s checkbox insertion strategy to run.
usages.contextHint: Prefer select/deSelect on enum-backed CheckboxUiElement in normal @Test code. This method is mainly for framework extensions/adapters/components.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the checkbox group represented by the element is selected and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .areSelected(CheckboxFields.NOTIFICATION_OPTIONS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe selected state across a checkbox group/container when your CheckboxUiElement represents a collection of checkboxes.
usages.contextHint: Use when your project models a checkbox group as a single CheckboxUiElement (e.g., container locator). For direct single-checkbox checks, use isSelected(...)/validateIsSelected(...).

items.summary: Validate that the checkbox is selected (checked), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .validateIsSelected(CheckboxFields.TERMS, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate selected state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Select/check the given checkbox and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .select(CheckboxFields.SUBSCRIBE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Check a checkbox (e.g., opt in to a feature or accept terms).
usages.contextHint: Prefer select(...) over direct WebElement clicks so the framework applies its checkbox component strategy and hooks.

items.summary: Retrieve selected checkbox value(s) for the element/group and store them into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .getSelected(CheckboxFields.NOTIFICATION_OPTIONS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture selected checkbox values when the test needs to reuse them later (e.g., compare with API payload, verify persisted settings).
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the selected values later from Quest storage. If you want an immediate assertion on a single checkbox, prefer validateIsSelected(...).

items.summary: Retrieve all checkbox value(s) for the element/group and store them into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .getAll(CheckboxFields.NOTIFICATION_OPTIONS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture all available checkbox values in a group/container when the test needs to validate availability or build data-driven assertions later.
usages.contextHint: Use when your CheckboxUiElement represents a group/container of checkboxes. Store is written to Quest storage for later use in the test flow.

items.summary: Validate that the checkbox is enabled (interactable), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .validateIsEnabled(CheckboxFields.SUBSCRIBE, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate enablement. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the checkbox is selected (checked) and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .checkbox()
  .isSelected(CheckboxFields.TERMS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe selected state when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether the checkbox is checked.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsSelected(...).

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring select (dropdown) elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s SelectService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., SelectFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed selects instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements SelectUiElement to centralize select locators and map each select to a concrete SelectComponentType (e.g., Bootstrap). The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific dropdown needs extra stabilization before selecting or validating options.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep select definitions in a single enum (e.g., SelectFields) and map each constant to the correct SelectComponentType so the framework selects the right dropdown implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic selects; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent SelectService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Alert behavior across different UI technologies/variants (visibility checks and reading message text). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the AlertComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Alert defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with alert/toast/message components. Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported alert technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding AlertComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Alert implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Checks whether an alert identified by the given locator is visible.

usages.description: Defines a locator-driven visibility check for alerts. Fluent services use this form when the alert is modeled by a stable selector rather than by container scoping.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: consider alert visibility nuances (presence vs displayed, animations, and timing) inside the concrete implementation. Test-side: prefer AlertServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the alert message text from an alert identified by the given locator.

usages.description: Defines a locator-driven value read for alerts. Fluent validations typically use this to assert success/error/information messages by reading the rendered alert text.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: normalize extracted text (trim/whitespace) and account for icons/hidden text if your alert markup includes them. Test-side: prefer AlertServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the alert message text from an alert resolved within the provided container element.

usages.description: Defines a container-scoped value read for alerts. This is useful when multiple alerts can exist on the page and the flow already narrowed scope to a specific section (modal/card/row).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep alert resolution relative to the container and aligned with your technology (CSS class, role='alert', etc.). Test-side: prefer AlertServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether an alert resolved within the provided container element is visible.

usages.description: Defines a container-scoped visibility check for alerts. Fluent services use this when alert interactions are scoped to a specific UI region to avoid matching a different alert instance elsewhere on the page.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep resolution relative to the container and consider animation/timing in visibility checks. Test-side: prefer AlertServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Table row-model annotation that describes how ROA locates and interprets a UI table (container, header row, and data rows). Apply this to your table row representation classes so TableServiceFluent can find the table on the page and map rows/cells into strongly-typed models.

usages.description: Annotate a row model with @TableInfo to define the table’s structural locators. ROA uses these locators to (1) find the table container, (2) enumerate row elements for reading/mapping, and (3) resolve header metadata for field-based mapping, filtering, and sorting. This enables stable table interactions without scattering table selectors throughout tests.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: put @TableInfo on each row representation class that ROA should map from a table (e.g., FilteredTransactionEntry). Choose locators that are stable and specific to that table instance. Framework-side: TableServiceFluent reads these annotation values to resolve the table container/header/rows and drive mapping logic. Test-side: you reference the row model via your TableElement/Tables enum and use TableServiceFluent to read/filter/sort rather than accessing annotation values directly.

items.summary: Defines the locator (FindBy) for the table header row, used to resolve column headers for field mapping, filtering, and sorting.

usages.description: headerRowLocator() tells ROA how to find the header row within the table container. The framework uses it to locate header cells, match columns to model fields, and support behaviors like sorting or header-based cell resolution when the widget requires it.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set headerRowLocator to the row element that contains your header cells (often thead tr). Framework-side: use this to resolve header cells deterministically for TableField projections and header-driven interactions. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent operations that rely on the row model mapping rather than inspecting this annotation value directly.

items.summary: Defines the locator (FindBy) for the table container element that scopes all table operations to the correct table instance.

usages.description: tableContainerLocator() tells ROA how to find the table container on the page (the root element for this table). The framework uses this container as the base scope for resolving header rows, data rows, and per-cell locators so it doesn’t accidentally interact with another table.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: choose a locator that uniquely identifies the table container (id/data-testid/container selector). Framework-side: resolve all subsequent row/header/cell lookups relative to this container to keep table operations safe on pages with multiple tables. Test-side: reference the table via your Tables/TableElement enum and use TableServiceFluent, not the annotation, to interact with it.

items.summary: Defines the locator (FindBy) for the table data rows inside the table container.

usages.description: rowsLocator() tells ROA how to enumerate the table’s row elements (typically tbody tr). The framework uses these rows as the basis for readRow/readTable operations and for locating per-row cells when mapping values into row models.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set rowsLocator to the selector that returns only data rows (exclude header/footer rows). Framework-side: use this to build stable row indexing and row iteration for mapping and criteria-based searches. Test-side: row reads are performed via TableServiceFluent; tests shouldn’t call or depend on rowsLocator() directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for tab controls (click, selected/visible/enabled checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides tab() -> TabServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab() using enum-backed TabUiElement registries.

usages.description: Expose TabServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the tab fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab(). Then interact with tabs using enum-backed TabUiElement constants (TabFields.*), finishing with drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: TabServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and should not be instantiated manually in tests. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.tab() (delegating to getTabField()). Test-side: prefer TabFields.* (TabUiElement) over raw By/WebElement.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is selected.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsSelected(TabFields.PROFILE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that the expected tab is currently selected.
usages.contextHint: Use when selected state must be true to proceed.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is not selected.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsNotSelected(TabFields.SETTINGS)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a tab is not currently selected.
usages.contextHint: Use when a tab must remain unselected as part of the scenario.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is disabled (not clickable).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsDisabled(TabFields.ADMIN)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a tab cannot be interacted with (e.g., due to permissions or state).
usages.contextHint: Use when disabled state must be true to proceed.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is hidden (not visible).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsHidden(TabFields.ADMIN)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a tab is not visible (e.g., restricted/role-based UI).
usages.contextHint: Use when hidden state is a strict requirement.

items.summary: Validate that the tab is not selected, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsNotSelected(TabFields.SETTINGS, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate non-selected state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Validate that the tab is visible, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate visible state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

usages.description: Validate enabled state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Validate that the tab is selected, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)

  .validateIsSelected(TabFields.PROFILE, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate selected state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is visible.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsVisible(TabFields.PROFILE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a tab is visible to the user.
usages.contextHint: Use when visibility must be true to proceed.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the tab is selected and store the result into Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .isSelected(TabFields.PROFILE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe selected state when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on the current tab selection.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsSelected(...).

usages.description: Validate disabled state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.

usages.description: Probe enabled state when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether the tab is interactable.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsEnabled(...).

usages.description: Probe visibility when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether the tab is present.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward tests, prefer validateIsVisible(...).
