usages.description: Validate hidden state. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the tab is enabled (clickable).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .validateIsEnabled(TabFields.PROFILE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a tab can be interacted with.
usages.contextHint: Use when enabled state must be true to proceed.

items.summary: Click the tab and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .tab()
  .click(TabFields.PROFILE)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Switch to a tab by clicking it using an enum-backed TabUiElement.
usages.contextHint: Prefer TabFields.* (TabUiElement) over raw By/WebElement. If tab selection triggers async loading, pair with loader waits where needed.

items.summary: Table row-model annotation that binds a TableCell field to a custom filter handler. Use this when a column cannot be filtered via the default “type into header filter input” logic (e.g., the filter requires selecting from a widget, clicking a header control, or applying a non-text filter interaction) during TableServiceFluent filter flows.

usages.description: Annotate a TableCell field inside a @TableInfo row model to override how ROA filters that column. Instead of default filtering, ROA instantiates the provided CellFilterFunction and calls it with the resolved header cell element, the FilterStrategy, and the filter values. This enables custom filter interactions such as dropdown selections, date pickers, multi-select filters, or applying filters via buttons/toggles.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: place @CustomCellFilter on the TableCell field that needs special filtering behavior and point cellFilterFunction() to a CellFilterFunction implementation (often a private static inner class near the field). Use alongside @TableCellLocator so the framework can resolve the correct header/cell for that column. Test-side: this is exercised through TableServiceFluent filter operations; tests provide values/strategy via the fluent API, not by calling the filter function directly.

items.summary: Defines the CellFilterFunction class that performs the custom filtering logic for the annotated TableCell field.

usages.description: Set cellFilterFunction() to a class implementing CellFilterFunction. During table filtering flows, ROA resolves the header cell for the annotated field and delegates filter execution to the handler, passing the header element, FilterStrategy, and provided values. This supports non-standard filter widgets that can’t be handled by default typing behavior.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep the filter function close to the TableCell definition (often as a private static inner class) and implement only the behavior needed for that specific column’s filter widget. Framework-side: instantiate the handler and invoke it with the resolved header cell element, strategy, and values. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() for filtering rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying ItemList components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the list technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching ItemList implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., ItemListComponentTypes) implementing ItemListComponentType to declare which list variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your ListUiElement field registry (e.g., ListFields) so each list locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right ItemList implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define ItemListComponentType enums to represent the list technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from ListFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete ItemList implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Checkbox behavior across different UI technologies/variants (select/deselect, discover labels, and verify selected/enabled state). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the CheckboxComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Checkbox defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with checkbox groups and individual checkbox controls. Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported checkbox technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding CheckboxComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Checkbox implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified top-level checkboxes (by label) are enabled.

usages.description: Label-based enabled checks are used by fluent validations when checkbox labels are stable and meaningful. The implementation must align with the checkbox technology (disabled attribute, aria-disabled, blocked label clicks, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define enabled semantics consistently (native disabled, aria-disabled, CSS state, etc.). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of checkbox labels identified within the container located by the given locator.

usages.description: Locator-based discovery is used when the fluent/service layer has a stable container selector but not an already-resolved SmartWebElement instance. Implementations typically delegate to the SmartWebElement overload to centralize label extraction logic.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to avoid duplicate label extraction logic. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more checkboxes by label within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped selection is used when checkbox groups appear multiple times on the page and label matching must be scoped to a specific UI region. Fluent services typically delegate to this method when selecting specific checkbox labels.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label resolution relative to the container and ensure selection is idempotent (only click when not already selected). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified top-level checkboxes (by label) are selected.

usages.description: Label-based selected checks are used by fluent validations to confirm checkbox state after selection or to assert default configuration. Implementations must align label resolution with select/deSelect behavior.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label-to-input resolution consistent with select/deSelect so assertions match interaction behavior. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified checkboxes by label are enabled within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped enabled checks are used when checkbox groups appear in repeated components (cards/rows/modals). Fluent validations delegate to this method to verify a set of checkboxes is interactive before acting on them.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: evaluate enabled state based on your technology (disabled attribute, aria-disabled, click-blocking wrappers). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects a checkbox chosen by Strategy within the provided container and returns the chosen label.

usages.description: Strategy-based deselection supports dynamic flows (e.g., unselect a random selected checkbox). Fluent services can use this for cleanup or exploratory scenarios where a specific label is not predetermined.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more top-level checkboxes by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global selection is useful when checkbox scope is unambiguous on the page. Fluent services typically prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple checkbox groups exist.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure global label matching is safe for your pages; prefer container-scoped logic when multiple groups exist. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of labels for all selected checkboxes within the provided container.

usages.description: Used to assert which options are currently selected or to drive dynamic deselection/cleanup flows. Fluent services typically expose this for validations and diagnostics.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label extraction consistent with select/deSelect and handle common checkbox markup patterns (label for/id, wrapped inputs). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of labels for all checkboxes within the provided container.

usages.description: Used to discover all checkbox options within a group for coverage checks and state validation. Fluent services can combine this with areEnabled/areSelected checks to validate configuration.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label extraction stable (trim/normalize) and consistent with how selection APIs address checkboxes by label. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified checkboxes by label are selected within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped selected checks are used when multiple checkbox groups exist on the page. Fluent validations delegate to this method to assert state for a specific group/region.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label-to-input resolution consistent with selection methods so assertions reflect real interactions. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of labels for all selected checkboxes identified within the container located by the given locator.

usages.description: Locator-based selected discovery is used when the fluent/service layer has a stable group/container selector. Implementations usually delegate to the SmartWebElement overload to keep behavior consistent.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to centralize label extraction and selection-state filtering. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether checkboxes identified by one or more locators are enabled.

usages.description: Locator-based enabled checks are used when labels are not reliable or when selectors directly reference checkbox inputs. Fluent validations can delegate to this in advanced scenarios where label matching is not appropriate.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators target the correct checkbox input/control and apply enabled semantics consistently. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more top-level checkboxes by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global deselection is useful when checkbox scope is unambiguous. Fluent services typically prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple groups exist or when cleanup must target a specific region.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep deselection idempotent (only click when selected) and prefer container-scoped logic on complex pages. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more checkboxes identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based deselection is used when selectors directly identify checkbox inputs (or labels are not reliable). Fluent services can use this variant in advanced cases where label-based APIs are not suitable.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep deselection idempotent and ensure locators refer to the intended checkbox control (input vs label). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more checkboxes by label within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped deselection is used to clean up or adjust state within a specific checkbox group/region. Fluent services delegate to this method when deselecting explicit labels within a scoped UI element.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep deselection idempotent (only click when selected) and scope all lookups to the container. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects a checkbox chosen by Strategy within the provided container and returns the chosen label.

usages.description: Strategy-based selection supports dynamic flows (e.g., select the first available checkbox, choose a random option). Fluent services use this for exploratory scenarios and generic setup steps.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether checkboxes identified by one or more locators are selected.

usages.description: Locator-based selected checks are used when selectors directly identify checkbox inputs or when labels are unreliable. Fluent validations can delegate to this method in advanced cases.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators target the correct checkbox input/control and keep selection semantics consistent with select/deSelect. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more checkboxes identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based selection is used when selectors directly identify checkbox inputs or when label-based matching is not appropriate. Fluent services can use this for advanced scenarios where locators provide the most stable targeting.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selection idempotent (only click when not selected) and ensure locators refer to the intended checkbox control. Test-side: prefer CheckboxServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).checkbox() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Input behavior across different UI technologies/variants (insert/clear, read values, check enabled state, and read validation/error messages). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the InputComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Input defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with input fields (text boxes and similar controls). Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported input technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding InputComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Input implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Inserts a value into the input field identified by label inside the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped insertion is used when multiple forms or repeated input blocks exist on the page and the same label may appear more than once. Fluent input flows typically delegate to this overload when interacting with labeled fields inside a specific region.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label-to-input resolution consistent and relative to the container; consider specialized input types (textarea, contenteditable) if your UI uses them. Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the validation/error message associated with the input field identified by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based error retrieval is used when input fields are best modeled by stable selectors and the service layer needs to assert validation feedback (required fields, format checks, server-side errors).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep error resolution aligned with your validation markup (e.g., Bootstrap invalid-feedback) and normalize extracted text. Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the current value of the input field identified by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based value retrieval is used by fluent validations and assertions when inputs are modeled by stable selectors. Implementations should consistently read values for different input types (input/textarea/etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: read values in a technology-appropriate way (value attribute for inputs/textarea, text for contenteditable if supported). Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the validation/error message for the input field identified by label inside the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped error retrieval is used when the same label can appear in multiple places and the flow needs validation feedback within a specific form/section. Fluent validations typically use this to assert field-level errors by label.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label resolution and error lookup relative to the container and aligned with your validation markup. Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the input field identified by locator is enabled.

usages.description: Locator-based enabled checks are used by fluent validations when a field is modeled by a stable selector. Implementations may need to handle disabled semantics beyond the native disabled attribute depending on the UI technology.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define enabled semantics consistently (native disabled, aria-disabled, read-only rules if considered non-interactive). Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clears the input field identified by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based clear is used when a field is identified by a stable selector. Fluent input flows typically use this to reset field state before inserting new values or to verify empty-state behavior.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: consider special input types that may require alternative clearing (masked inputs, contenteditable, JS-driven fields). Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the current value of the input field identified by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based value retrieval is used when the input scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations may prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple forms exist on the same page.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global label matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped resolution to avoid reading the wrong field on complex pages. Test-side: prefer InputServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).input() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Applies a table header filter using an input field within the provided table container.

items.summary: Fluent service for button controls (click, visibility/enabled checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides button() -> ButtonServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() using enum-backed ButtonUiElement registries.

usages.description: Expose ButtonServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the button fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button(). Then interact with buttons using enum-backed ButtonUiElement constants (ButtonFields.*), finishing with drop() and complete().

items.summary: Hard-assert that a button is disabled (not clickable).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .button()
  .validateIsDisabled(ButtonFields.SUBMIT_BUTTON)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a button is disabled. Useful when the UI should block progression until prerequisites are met (e.g., required fields, permissions, or validation rules).
usages.contextHint: Use when disabled state is a strict requirement. If you need to validate multiple buttons/states in one flow, use the boolean overload to aggregate results.

items.summary: Validate that a button is disabled (not clickable), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .button()
  .validateIsDisabled(ButtonFields.SUBMIT_BUTTON, true)
  .validateIsDisabled(ButtonFields.DELETE_BUTTON, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate disabled state for one or more buttons. When soft=true, failures are aggregated (useful for "verify whole form state" checks); when soft=false, it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that a button is visible to the user.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .button()
  .validateIsVisible(ButtonFields.SIGN_IN_BUTTON)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a button is present and visible. Useful for verifying navigation outcomes and role-based UI rendering (buttons shown only when the user is allowed to act).
usages.contextHint: Use when visibility is a strict requirement. If you need to validate multiple buttons/sections in one flow, use the boolean overload to aggregate results.

items.summary: Hard-assert that a button is enabled (clickable).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)

  .validateIsEnabled(ButtonFields.SIGN_IN_BUTTON)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a button can be clicked. Useful when a flow requires a button to become interactable after user input or when state changes are expected.
usages.contextHint: Use when enabled state is a strict precondition. If you need aggregated checks across multiple buttons, use the boolean overload.

items.summary: Hard-assert that a button is hidden (not visible).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)

  .validateIsHidden(ButtonFields.DELETE_BUTTON)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that a button is not shown. Useful for negative permission checks (actions not available), feature flags, or state-dependent UI (button appears only after a step).
usages.contextHint: Use when the button must not be visible in the current state/role. If validating multiple hidden controls, use the boolean overload to aggregate results.

items.summary: Evaluate whether a button is enabled and store the result into Quest storage.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Alert components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the alert technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Alert implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., AlertComponentTypes) implementing AlertComponentType to declare which alert variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your AlertUiElement field registry (e.g., AlertFields) so each alert locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Alert implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define AlertComponentType enums to represent the alert technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from AlertFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Alert implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Test-side method annotation that enables network request interception during UI tests by declaring one or more request URL substrings to capture. Intercepted requests/responses can then be validated or extracted via the UI interceptor fluent service.

items.summary: Defines the list of request URL substrings (as strings) that should be intercepted during the annotated test method.

items.summary: Framework-side functional contract for custom table cell insertion. usages.description: Implement CellInsertionFunction to define how ROA should “insert” into a specific table cell when default typing/selecting is insufficient. usages.contextHint: Project-side: implement this interface as the insertionFunction referenced by @CustomCellInsertion on a TableCell field in a @TableInfo row model. Framework-side: invoke the function during TableServiceFluent insert/edit operations after resolving the target cell element. Test-side: this runs indirectly through table fluent flows; tests should not instantiate or call these functions directly.

items.summary: BiConsumer-style default entry point that delegates to cellInsertionFunction(...) for the actual cell insertion behavior.

usages.description: accept(...) exists to allow CellInsertionFunction to be used as a BiConsumer-like callback. In practice, you implement cellInsertionFunction(...); the default accept(...) delegates to it so the framework can invoke the handler uniformly.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: call accept(cell, values) when integrating with BiConsumer-based pipelines; it should forward to cellInsertionFunction(...) to keep a single implementation point. Project-side: implement cellInsertionFunction(...), not accept(...). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Custom insertion hook that performs the actual interaction needed to populate a table cell, using the resolved cell element and optional values.

items.summary: Checks whether the radio option identified by label is enabled at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based enabled checks are used when radio scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations may prefer container-scoped overloads on complex pages with multiple groups.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global label matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped resolution on complex pages. Test-side: prefer RadioServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the radio option identified by label is visible at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based visibility checks are used when radio scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations may prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple groups exist.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global label matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped resolution when multiple groups exist. Test-side: prefer RadioServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects the radio option identified by label at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based selection is used when radio scope is unambiguous. Fluent services typically prefer container-scoped selection on pages with multiple radio groups to avoid selecting an option from the wrong group.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selection idempotent and use global label matching only when safe. Test-side: prefer RadioServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the label/text of the currently selected radio option within the provided container.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations and branching flows to determine the currently selected option inside a specific radio group/region. Implementations must reliably locate the selected input and map it back to a stable label.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selected detection and label extraction consistent with getAll()/select() label semantics. Test-side: prefer RadioServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for table components: read full tables/row ranges, read specific rows, filter and sort columns, click actions inside cells, insert/update cell values, and validate stored table snapshots. All operations are driven by a project enum implementing TableElement (e.g., Tables.OUTFLOW) which declares the row representation class and optional before/after hooks for stabilization.

usages.description: Expose TableServiceFluent via your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests can operate on tables through quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table(). The table itself is represented by a project enum constant (Tables.OUTFLOW) implementing TableElement, which provides the row model mapping and optional hooks used by the table implementation.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: model each table as an enum implementing TableElement<T> (e.g., Tables) and provide row representation class + optional before/after hooks (stabilization waits). Test-side: pass Tables.<TABLE> to all table() methods; use TableField.of(RowModel::setter) to describe which columns to read/filter/sort.

items.summary: Sort a table column (TableField) using a SortingStrategy (ASC/DESC/NO_SORT) and continue the fluent chain.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .sortTable(Tables.OUTFLOW, TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory), SortingStrategy.ASC)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Apply sorting on a column described by a TableField mapping (typically TableField.of(RowModel::setter)). This is used to make subsequent reads/validations deterministic or to verify sorting behavior explicitly.
usages.contextHint: Use the same TableField mapping style used for reads (TableField.of(RowModel::setter)). SortingStrategy is ASC/DESC/NO_SORT.

items.summary: Read the entire table represented by the TableElement and store the snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readTable(Tables.OUTFLOW)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a full table snapshot (all rows/columns as defined by the table implementation + row representation class) so you can validate it later or use its data in the same flow.
usages.contextHint: The snapshot is stored under the table key (Tables.OUTFLOW.enumImpl()) in UI sub-storage. Use validate(table, assertions...) to assert against stored snapshots.

items.summary: Read a range of rows from the table (fromRow..toRow) and limit the captured snapshot to the provided TableField mappings, then store the result in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readTable(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3, 5,
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount)
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Read only a slice of the table and only the fields you care about. This matches how tables are typically asserted in tests: you focus on a row window and specific columns rather than full rows.
usages.contextHint: The last parameter is varargs in test usage (compiled as TableField[]). Use TableField.of(RowModel::setter) to specify which columns to read. Row indexes follow your table implementation’s indexing (commonly 1-based in these fluent APIs).

items.summary: Read the entire table but only the specified TableField columns, then store the snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readTable(Tables.OUTFLOW,
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount)
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture only specific columns across the whole table. Useful when you want to validate a few relevant fields without reading full row content.
usages.contextHint: Use TableField.of(RowModel::setter) mappings to define columns. This keeps the stored snapshot focused and makes later validations simpler.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and click the Nth clickable element inside the target column cell.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .clickElementInCell(
      Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS,
      List.of("Starbucks"),
      TableField.of(AllTransactionEntry::setDescription),
      1
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use this when a cell contains multiple clickable controls (e.g., action links/buttons inside a cell) and you need to click a specific one by index after locating the row via criteria.
usages.contextHint: Row criteria is interpreted by your table implementation to find the matching row. The click index targets the Nth element in that cell (commonly 1-based).

items.summary: Read a single row by row index and store the row snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readRow(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a single row when your test scenario targets a specific row position (often after applying sorting/filtering).
usages.contextHint: Prefer criteria-based row targeting when ordering is unstable. Row indexing follows your table implementation (commonly 1-based).

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and insert/update value(s) in a target cell (column).

usages.description: Use this to update an editable cell (input/select) inside a table row located by content rather than index. This keeps tests stable when sorting/pagination changes row positions.
usages.contextHint: Values are passed as String[] to support components that may require multiple values. Criteria matching and insertion mechanics depend on your table implementation and column type.

items.summary: Read a range of rows from the table (fromRow..toRow) and store the snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readTable(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3, 5)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a subset of rows when only a specific window is relevant (e.g., recent records, top results).
usages.contextHint: Use the overload with TableField mappings when you want to limit captured columns. Row indexing follows your table implementation (commonly 1-based).

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and click the primary clickable element inside the target column cell.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .clickElementInCell(
      Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS,
      List.of("Starbucks"),
      TableField.of(AllTransactionEntry::setDescription)
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use this when the cell has a single actionable element (or your table implementation treats the first as primary) and you don’t need a specific index.
usages.contextHint: Criteria is used to locate the row; TableField targets the column cell. Use the overload with an index when multiple clickable elements exist in the same cell.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria, read only the specified TableField columns, and store the row snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readRow(
      Tables.OUTFLOW,
      List.of("Food"),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount)
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use this for stable reads in dynamic tables: locate the row by content and capture only the columns you need for assertions.
usages.contextHint: Criteria matching is driven by your table implementation. Use TableField.of(RowModel::setter) for each column you want to read.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and click a cell element using a typed row model (K) to drive matching/targeting.

usages.description: Use a row model object when your table implementation supports model-driven targeting for cell actions (e.g., mapping model fields to columns/actions).
usages.contextHint: K should match the table’s row representation class (Tables.OUTFLOW uses OutFlow.class). Prefer this overload when your project treats table rows as typed objects and leverages that mapping.

items.summary: Click a cell element in a row identified by row index using a typed row model (K) to drive targeting.

usages.description: Use when the row position is known and you still want model-driven targeting for the click behavior (depending on your table implementation).
usages.contextHint: Row index follows your table implementation (commonly 1-based). Prefer criteria-based targeting when sorting/filtering can reorder rows.

items.summary: Validate the stored table snapshot (previously captured via readTable/readRow) against the provided assertions.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readTable(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3, 5,
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount)
  )
  .validate(
      Tables.OUTFLOW,
      Assertion.builder().type(TableAssertionTypes.TABLE_NOT_EMPTY).expected(true).build()
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Read table data into Quest storage and then assert against that stored snapshot using table assertion types (e.g., not empty, row count, uniqueness, column rules).
usages.contextHint: This validates against the snapshot stored under the TableElement key (Tables.OUTFLOW.enumImpl()). Ensure you have readTable/readRow before validate(...) so there is data in storage.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and insert/update cell values using a typed row model (K).

usages.description: Use a row model to update multiple mapped cells in one call when your table implementation supports model-to-column insertion.
usages.contextHint: K should match the table’s row representation class. Prefer this when you already have a row model object and want the framework to map its fields to table columns.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria, read it, and store the row snapshot in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readRow(Tables.OUTFLOW, List.of("Food"))
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use this when table ordering is dynamic: locate the row by content and capture its data for later validation/usage.
usages.contextHint: Criteria interpretation is defined by your table implementation. This is usually the most stable way to read a specific row in a dynamic table.

items.summary: Insert/update cell values in a specific row (by row index) using a typed row model (K).

usages.description: Update a known row position using model-driven mapping into editable cells (depending on your table implementation).
usages.contextHint: Row index follows your table implementation (commonly 1-based). Prefer criteria-based overload when sorting/filtering can reorder rows.

items.summary: Click the primary clickable element inside the specified column cell for a given row index.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .clickElementInCell(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3, TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory))
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Click the default/primary element inside the cell at the given row and column (useful for tables where a cell links to details).
usages.contextHint: Use the overload with element index when the cell contains multiple clickable elements. Row index follows your table implementation.

items.summary: Read a row by index while limiting the snapshot to specific TableField columns, then store it in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .readRow(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3,
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount)
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture a single row by index but only the columns needed for the scenario (keeps the snapshot focused for assertions).
usages.contextHint: Row index follows your table implementation (commonly 1-based). Use criteria-based readRow when row ordering is not stable.

items.summary: Filter a table column using a FilterStrategy (SELECT_ONLY/SELECT/SELECT_ALL/UNSELECT/UNSELECT_ALL) and one or more values.

usages.description: Apply a column filter through the table’s filter UI. Strategy controls whether you replace existing selections, add, remove, select all, or clear all.
usages.contextHint: FilterStrategy is an enum. Values should match the visible labels used by the filter UI for the given column.

items.summary: Locate a row by search criteria and insert into a specific editable element (by index) inside the target cell (column).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .insertCellValue(
      Tables.OUTFLOW,
      List.of("Food"),
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount),
      1,
      new String[]{ "120" }
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Use when a column cell contains multiple editors/controls and you need to target the Nth one for insertion.
usages.contextHint: Element index targets the Nth editor inside the cell (commonly 1-based). Prefer simpler overloads when the cell contains a single editor/control.

items.summary: Insert values into a specific editable element (by index) inside a column cell for a specific row (by row index).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .insertCellValue(
      Tables.OUTFLOW,
      3,
      TableField.of(OutFlow::setAmount),
      1,
      new String[]{ "120" }
  )
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Target a known row index and a specific editor index inside the cell when the cell contains multiple controls.
usages.contextHint: Row index and element index follow your table implementation (commonly 1-based). Prefer criteria-based overloads when row ordering can change.

items.summary: Insert values into the specified column cell for a row identified by row index.

usages.description: Update a cell in a known row position (common for inline editable tables) using a TableField mapping for the column.
usages.contextHint: Use this overload when the cell contains a single editor/control. Use String[] to support components that accept multiple values.

items.summary: Click the Nth clickable element inside a column cell for a specific row index.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .table()
  .clickElementInCell(Tables.OUTFLOW, 3, TableField.of(OutFlow::setCategory), 1)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Click a specific action/control in a cell when the cell contains multiple clickable elements and you need deterministic targeting by index.
usages.contextHint: Row index and click index follow your table implementation (commonly 1-based). Use this overload for action-heavy cells (multiple links/buttons).

items.summary: Table row-model field annotation that declares a column as filterable by delegating to a UI component implementation (based on component interface type + concrete componentType identifier). Use this when filtering is performed via a specific component widget in the header (input/select/datepicker/etc.) rather than generic table filtering.

usages.description: Annotate a TableCell with @CellFilter to tell ROA which component contract should perform filtering for that column. The framework uses type() to know which component interface to delegate to (e.g., InputComponentType) and componentType() to resolve the concrete implementation (via @ImplementationOfType). This enables technology-specific filtering behavior for columns whose header filter UI is implemented as an input/select/etc.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: place @CellFilter on the TableCell field and pair it with @TableCellLocator so ROA can resolve both the header cell and the data cell for the column. Use type() to declare which component interface performs the filtering and componentType() to specify the concrete implementation identifier. Framework-side: resolve the implementation via @ImplementationOfType and apply FilterStrategy/value insertion through the resolved component. Test-side: filtering is exercised through TableServiceFluent filter operations; tests should not read annotation values directly.

items.summary: Declares the component type interface used to perform filtering for this column (e.g., InputComponentType, SelectComponentType).

usages.description: type() identifies which component contract ROA should delegate to when applying a filter for the annotated column. The framework uses this interface type as the dispatch point to route filtering behavior to the correct component implementation family.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: choose the component interface that matches the header filter widget (input/select/etc.). Framework-side: use type() together with componentType() to resolve the correct @ImplementationOfType-backed implementation and apply filter values using FilterStrategy. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() for filtering rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Specifies the concrete component type identifier used to resolve the filtering implementation (matched by @ImplementationOfType).

usages.description: componentType() provides the concrete identifier (usually a constant from a project enum Data class) that ROA uses to resolve the correct implementation via @ImplementationOfType. This ensures filtering behavior matches the UI technology/variant used by that column’s header filter widget.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set componentType() to the identifier that corresponds to the component implementation class annotated with @ImplementationOfType. Framework-side: resolve implementations by matching this identifier so the correct filter behavior is executed. Test-side: filtering runs through TableServiceFluent; tests should not call component implementations directly.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring tab elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s TabService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., TabFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed tabs instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements TabUiElement to centralize tab locators and map each tab to a concrete TabComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific tab needs extra stabilization before clicking or validating selection/visibility.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep tab definitions in a single enum (e.g., TabFields) and map each constant to the correct TabComponentType so the framework selects the right tab implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic tabs; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent TabService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Checkbox components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the checkbox technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Checkbox implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., CheckboxComponentTypes) implementing CheckboxComponentType to declare which checkbox variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your CheckboxUiElement field registry (e.g., CheckboxFields) so each checkbox locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Checkbox implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define CheckboxComponentType enums to represent the checkbox technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from CheckboxFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Checkbox implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Table row-model field annotation that defines how ROA locates a specific column’s cell (within a row) and its corresponding header cell. Apply this to TableCell fields inside a @TableInfo row model so the framework can resolve the correct cell/header for reading, filtering, sorting, and custom behaviors.

usages.description: Use @TableCellLocator on a TableCell field to bind a row-model property to a concrete table column. ROA uses cellLocator to resolve the cell inside each row and headerCellLocator to resolve the matching column header, enabling stable column mapping, header-driven operations, and consistent row-to-model extraction.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: annotate each TableCell field in your @TableInfo row model with the cell locator (relative to a row) and the header locator (relative to the header row or container). Framework-side: use these locators to resolve cells/headers deterministically for mapping and column-level behaviors. Test-side: tests interact via TableServiceFluent and row models; they should not read annotation values directly.

items.summary: Optional section identifier used when a table is composed of multiple logical sections (e.g., multiple header/body blocks or segmented tables) and the same column indices/locators would otherwise be ambiguous.

usages.description: tableSection() allows the framework to disambiguate column resolution when the table has multiple sections that share similar row/cell structures (e.g., summary vs details sections rendered in separate blocks). If your table is a single, standard header + body structure, you can omit tableSection and rely on the default behavior.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set tableSection only when you truly have multiple table sections that would cause ambiguous cell/header resolution; otherwise omit it to keep models simple. Framework-side: treat tableSection as an optional discriminator when resolving the correct header/row scope for a column. Test-side: the section selection is applied automatically through the mapped row model during TableServiceFluent operations.

items.summary: Defines the locator (FindBy) for the corresponding header cell of this column, used for header-driven mapping and operations such as filtering and sorting.

usages.description: headerCellLocator() tells ROA how to find the header cell for the annotated column. The framework can use it to match a TableCell to a specific header, resolve header-based widgets (filter inputs, sort triggers), and keep mapping stable even if columns shift visually.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set headerCellLocator to the header cell that represents the same column as cellLocator. Framework-side: use this header reference for sorting/filtering/column resolution when the table widget requires header interactions. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent operations (filterTable/sortTable/read projections) rather than interacting with header elements directly.

items.summary: Optional locator (FindBy) for extracting text/value inside the resolved cell when the cell container isn’t the actual text source (e.g., nested span, link, badge).

usages.description: cellTextLocator() lets you point ROA at the exact element within the cell that contains the readable value. If omitted, ROA typically reads text from the resolved cell element itself, which is sufficient for simple cells but not for cells containing multiple nested elements or decorative wrappers.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: use cellTextLocator only when the cell’s direct text is not the value you want (e.g., nested spans, icons + text, link wrappers). Framework-side: prefer this locator for value extraction while keeping cellLocator responsible for locating the column cell container. Test-side: value extraction is handled by TableServiceFluent reads/mapping; tests shouldn’t manually parse cells.

items.summary: Defines the locator (FindBy) for the column cell within a table row (typically relative to the row element).

usages.description: cellLocator() tells ROA how to find the cell for this column within each row. The framework uses it during row mapping to extract values and during insert/edit flows to resolve the target cell for interactions.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set cellLocator as a row-relative selector that reliably points to the correct column cell (e.g., td:nth-of-type(n), td.date, [data-col='date']). Framework-side: resolve this locator relative to the row element to keep mapping stable across multiple tables. Test-side: cell resolution is driven by row models and TableServiceFluent operations, not by calling annotation methods.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring loader/spinner elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s LoaderService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., LoaderFields) so UI waits/validations can target strongly-typed loaders instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements LoaderUiElement to centralize loader locators and map each loader to a concrete LoaderComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific loader needs extra stabilization before asserting visibility or waiting for it to disappear.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep loader definitions in a single enum (e.g., LoaderFields) and map each constant to the correct LoaderComponentType so the framework selects the right loader implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic loaders; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent LoaderService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Used inside @Journey(journeyData=...) to declare which test data model(s) should be prepared and passed as arguments to the journey before the test body runs. The value is typically written as a constant from a project enum implementing DataForge (e.g., DataCreator implements DataForge<DataCreator>).

usages.description: Default (eager) journey data: the framework resolves the model referenced by JourneyData.value and passes the created object(s) into the journey precondition as arguments. In real projects, JourneyData.value is commonly written as a constant from an enum that implements DataForge (e.g., DataCreator implements DataForge<DataCreator>).
usages.contextHint: Use only inside @Journey(journeyData = {...}) on a @Test method. Include journeyData only when the journey requires input models; omit it when the journey takes no inputs. The value must reference a project-defined model key (string), typically coming from your DataForge enum.

items.summary: Deferred value wrapper used to delay creating a crafted test-data object until you explicitly call create(). Commonly used as a @Test parameter together with @Craft(model = "...") when the model should be materialized on demand.

usages.description: Use Late<T> when you want the crafted model to be created on demand. Call create() at the moment you need the real object (useful when creation should happen after navigation/login/preconditions).
usages.contextHint: Typically used as a @Test parameter together with @Craft(model = "..."). create() materializes the actual object; until then, no model is created.

items.summary: Project-side contract for reusable preconditions (journeys) executed before the test body. Implement it as an enum (e.g., Preconditions) where each constant maps to a BiConsumer<SuperQuest, Object[]> function. Tests trigger these journeys via @Journey(value = "<KEY>", ...) and optionally pass inputs via @JourneyData("<MODEL_KEY>").

usages.description: Define a project enum implementing PreQuestJourney to register reusable setup logic. The framework resolves @Journey.value to a Preconditions entry, executes its journey() function before the test body, and passes any @JourneyData-resolved models via the Object[] arguments.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep each journey deterministic and safe to run as setup; use the Object[] args only when the journey needs crafted inputs. Test-side: reference the journey by its string key in @Journey(value = "...") and include @JourneyData only when the journey expects input models.

items.summary: Base test class for ROA-style UI tests. Extend it for the default (parallel-friendly) execution model and to use built-in storage retrieval helpers (retrieve / hookData) that read values written by rings, interceptors, hooks, and test-data providers.

usages.description: Extend BaseQuest for standard test classes. Tests inject Quest as a method parameter and use quest.use(...) to activate rings. BaseQuest also provides convenience retrieval helpers for values stored during the quest flow.
usages.contextHint: Use BaseQuest for normal test classes. Use BaseQuestSequential only when tests must run sequentially and/or share state across methods.

items.summary: Retrieve a typed object from the default quest storage namespace using a single enum key.

usages.description: Use when a ring, interceptor, or framework helper stores a value under a single enum key in the default storage map and you want to read it back in a typed way inside the test flow.
usages.contextHint: Use for simple “one-key” retrieval from the default storage namespace. Prefer DataExtractor-based retrieval when you’re reading from structured storage patterns (static test data, intercepted payload extraction, etc.).

items.summary: Retrieve a typed value using a DataExtractor (recommended for framework-managed storage patterns like static test data or intercepted/extracted payloads).

usages.description: Use a DataExtractor to read a value from a structured storage source (e.g., static test data loaded by @StaticTestData). The extractor encapsulates where/how the value is stored so your test stays clean and consistent.
usages.contextHint: Common pairings: @StaticTestData(...) + staticTestData(...). DataExtractor-based retrieval is also a good fit for interceptor/extraction utilities that write derived values to storage.

items.summary: Retrieve a typed value produced by class-level hooks (e.g., DB/API/UI hooks) from the hook-scoped storage map using a key object (often an enum).

usages.description: Use hookData(...) to read values written by hook flows (BEFORE/AFTER class hooks) into the dedicated hook-scoped storage map, so you can reuse hook-prepared artifacts during your test.
usages.contextHint: Hook-side requirement: the hook must store a value using the same key object (commonly an enum key) via its hook storage map. Prefer hookData(...) for hook outputs instead of retrieve(...), since hooks use a dedicated hook-scoped storage.

items.summary: Retrieve an indexed value using a DataExtractor when multiple values were stored under the same extractor key (index is 1-based from the end: 1 = latest, 2 = previous).

usages.code:
// After running the same intercepted/extracted flow multiple times, you can compare latest vs previous
List<String> latest = retrieve(
    DataExtractorFunctions.responseBodyExtraction(
        RequestsInterceptor.INTERCEPT_REQUEST_AUTH.getEndpointSubString(),
        "$[0].changes[?(@.key=='totalPrice')].value",
        "for(;;);"),
    1,
    List.class);

List<String> previous = retrieve(
    DataExtractorFunctions.responseBodyExtraction(
        RequestsInterceptor.INTERCEPT_REQUEST_AUTH.getEndpointSubString(),
        "$[0].changes[?(@.key=='totalPrice')].value",
        "for(;;);"),
    2,
    List.class);
usages.description: Use indexed retrieval when the same extractor target is stored more than once in a single test flow (e.g., repeated interceptions or repeated extraction points). This is useful for comparisons like “value changed after edit.”
usages.contextHint: Indexing is 1-based from the end: 1 = latest stored value, 2 = previous stored value. Requires that the extractor target was stored at least as many times as the index you request.

items.summary: Retrieve a typed object from a sub-storage namespace using a namespace key (subKey) and an entry key.

usages.description: Use when setup/preconditions or framework flows store values into a dedicated namespace (sub-storage) and you need to read them back by (namespace, entryKey). A common example is precondition arguments stored under PRE_ARGUMENTS keyed by the DataForge enum constant.
usages.contextHint: Use after the producing flow has executed (e.g., a @Journey precondition that stores its input/output under PRE_ARGUMENTS). This keeps data sharing explicit without passing objects manually through every call.

items.summary: Table row-model field annotation that declares how ROA should insert values into a specific TableCell by delegating to a UI component implementation (based on component interface type + concrete componentType identifier). Use this when inserting into the cell requires a specific component behavior (e.g., Link, Button, Input) rather than generic cell text handling.

usages.description: Annotate a TableCell with @CellInsertion to tell ROA which component contract should perform insertion for that cell. The framework uses type() to know which component interface to delegate to (e.g., LinkComponentType) and componentType() to resolve the concrete implementation (via @ImplementationOfType). This enables consistent, technology-specific insertion behavior for complex cells (links/buttons/inputs inside table cells).
usages.contextHint: Project-side: place @CellInsertion on the TableCell field and pair it with @TableCellLocator so the framework can resolve the target cell. Use type() to declare which component interface performs the insertion and componentType() to specify the concrete implementation identifier. order() is optional and defaults to 0—set it only when multiple insertions in the same row must run in a specific sequence. Test-side: this is executed through TableServiceFluent insert/edit flows; tests should not access annotation values directly.

items.summary: Declares the component type interface used to perform insertion for this cell (e.g., LinkComponentType, InputComponentType).

usages.description: type() identifies which component contract ROA should delegate to when inserting into the annotated TableCell. The framework uses this interface type as the dispatch point to route insertion behavior to the correct component implementation family.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: choose the component interface that matches the interaction needed in the cell (link/button/input/select/etc.). Framework-side: use type() together with componentType() to resolve and invoke the correct @ImplementationOfType-backed implementation. Test-side: this routing happens automatically during TableServiceFluent insert/edit operations.

items.summary: Specifies the concrete component type identifier used to resolve the implementation (matched by @ImplementationOfType).

usages.description: componentType() provides the concrete identifier (usually a constant from a project enum Data class) that ROA uses to resolve the correct implementation via @ImplementationOfType. This ensures the insertion behavior matches the UI technology/variant (e.g., Bootstrap link).
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set componentType() to the identifier that corresponds to your implementation class annotated with @ImplementationOfType. Framework-side: resolve implementations by matching this identifier so the correct component behavior is used inside the table cell. Test-side: table insertion/edit flows rely on this mapping automatically.

items.summary: Optional execution order for the insertion when multiple @CellInsertion handlers exist in the same row model. If omitted, it defaults to 0.

usages.description: Use order() to define a deterministic sequence when multiple cell insertions in the same row require a specific ordering (e.g., click a link first, then insert text into a dependent cell). If you don’t specify order, it is treated as 0, which is sufficient for most cases.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set order only when insertion sequencing matters; otherwise omit it and rely on the default (0). Framework-side: execute insertions in order value sequence to keep row insert/edit flows deterministic. Test-side: ordering is applied automatically during TableServiceFluent insert/edit operations.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Table components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare table technologies/variants when needed, while most projects can rely on DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT for standard tables that don’t require a custom implementation.

usages.description: Use TableComponentType to classify how a table should be handled by ROA. In most cases you can use DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT (or omit the type entirely and let TableElement provide the default). Your table registry (e.g., Tables implementing TableElement) primarily standardizes table interactions by binding each table to a strongly-typed row model, with optional before/after hooks for synchronization.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: tables are typically declared via a TableElement enum (e.g., Tables) that binds each table to a row representation class and optionally a TableComponentType. If you use DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT (or leave the type null), there’s no need for a dedicated table implementation class—ROA uses the default table behavior. Only introduce additional table types (and corresponding @ImplementationOfType bindings) when you truly need specialized table behavior beyond the default.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring radio elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s RadioService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., RadioFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed radio controls instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements RadioUiElement to centralize radio locators and map each radio to a concrete RadioComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific radio control needs extra stabilization before selecting or validating selection state.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep radio definitions in a single enum (e.g., RadioFields) and map each constant to the correct RadioComponentType so the framework selects the right radio implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic radio controls; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent RadioService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Project-side contract for reusable test-data models used by @Craft and @JourneyData. Implement it as an enum (e.g., DataCreator) where each constant provides a Late creator for a specific model, and annotations reference the model by its string key (e.g., "PURCHASE_CURRENCY").

usages.description: Define a DataForge enum (DataCreator) to register a reusable model factory, then use @Craft(model = "...") to inject a ready instance as a test parameter. The framework resolves the model key to the matching DataForge entry and creates the object before the test body runs.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep DataForge entries focused and reusable (builders/factories), returning Late so the framework can support eager or deferred creation patterns. Test-side: use @Craft(model = "PURCHASE_CURRENCY") to inject the model without hardcoding setup in the test body.

usages.description: Use the same DataForge model key in @JourneyData when a precondition needs input models before the test body runs. The journey implementation receives the resolved object(s) as its arguments and can store outputs for later retrieval.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: DataCreator (DataForge) remains the single registry for reusable models. Test-side: reference the same string key in @JourneyData that you would use in @Craft so preconditions can receive the crafted input models.

items.summary: Concrete RetryCondition implementation. Create it by providing a function (produces value) and a predicate (success condition). Used with FluentService.retryUntil(...) via a project ring wrapper method.

usages.description: Create a simple retry condition that succeeds when the condition becomes true (e.g., an element is present/visible).
usages.contextHint: Use as input to a ring method that wraps FluentService.retryUntil(condition, maxWait, retryInterval, service). The function is called repeatedly until the predicate returns true or timeout occurs.

usages.code:
RetryCondition<Boolean> condition = new RetryConditionImpl<>(
    driver -> driver.find(By.cssSelector(".dashboard")).isDisplayed(),
    result -> result
);

quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
    .browser().navigate(getUiConfig().baseUrl())
    .retryUntil(condition, Duration.ofSeconds(10), Duration.ofSeconds(1))
    .drop()
    .use(RING_OF_UI)
    .button().click(DashboardButtons.REFRESH)
    .drop()
    .complete();
usages.description: Use the created RetryConditionImpl via a ring wrapper that calls FluentService.retryUntil(...), then continue the fluent chain after it succeeds.
usages.contextHint: retryUntil(...) runs before the next chain steps. Keep the condition focused; after it passes, proceed with normal UI actions/validations. The wrapper method should pass the correct service object (e.g., SmartWebDriver) into the condition’s function.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Tab behavior across different UI technologies/variants. Tab extends Button, so implementations must provide all Button operations (click/state checks, etc.) plus tab-specific behavior (select/click by label/locator and verify selected state, optionally within a container). Implementations are resolved at runtime via the TabComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Tab defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with tab controls (switching tabs and asserting which tab is active). Because Tab extends Button, implementations must also provide the inherited Button behavior so tabs can be treated as clickable controls consistently across technologies.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported tab technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding TabComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Tab implementation at runtime. Because Tab extends Button, ensure your implementation overrides the full Button method set in addition to Tab-specific methods so runtime delegation is complete and consistent.

items.summary: Checks whether the tab identified by label is selected at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global label-based selected checks are used when tab scope is unambiguous on the page. Fluent validations typically delegate to this method for asserting active tabs by name.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement selected detection based on your tab technology (aria-selected, active class, selected state on parent li, etc.). Test-side: prefer TabServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the tab identified by label is selected within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped selected checks are used when multiple tab sets exist on the same page and label matching must be scoped to a specific UI region. Fluent validations typically delegate to this method to avoid matching the wrong tab group.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label resolution relative to the container and align selected detection with your tab technology (aria-selected, active class, etc.). Test-side: prefer TabServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the tab identified by locator is selected.

usages.description: Locator-based selected checks are used when a tab has a stable selector and label matching is not desired. Fluent validations delegate to this method in such scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure the locator targets the correct selectable tab element and keep selected detection aligned with the technology’s active-state semantics. Test-side: prefer TabServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the tab resolved inside the provided container is selected.

usages.description: Used when the flow already narrowed scope to a specific tab element container and needs to assert its active/selected state. Fluent validations typically use this in component-scoped checks where the tab element is already resolved.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure the container-based resolution targets the intended tab element (role='tab', active link) for your technology. Test-side: prefer TabServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).tab() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for radio controls (select, read selected value/options, visibility/enabled/disabled/hidden checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides radio() -> RadioServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio() using enum-backed RadioUiElement registries (RadioFields.*) instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Expose RadioServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so test code can enter the radio fluent chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).radio(). Then interact with radio controls using enum-backed RadioUiElement constants (RadioFields.*) for selecting and validating state, finishing with drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: RadioServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and uses the configured radio component implementation; do not instantiate it manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.radio() (delegating to getRadioField()). Test-side: prefer RadioFields.* (RadioUiElement) to centralize locators/component typing (and optional hooks) in one place.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the available radio option values for the given RadioUiElement match the expected set.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .radio()
  .validateAllRadioInputs(RadioFields.GENDER_GROUP, new String[]{ "MALE", "FEMALE" })
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Verify the full set of radio options for a radio group (e.g., choices rendered for a user) using a project enum element that represents the group/container.
usages.contextHint: Use a RadioUiElement that models the radio group/container when validating the full option list. Keep expected option labels/values aligned with what the component exposes (visible text/value attribute, depending on implementation).

items.summary: Evaluate whether the radio element is visible and store the boolean result in Quest storage.

usages.code:

  .radio()
  .isVisible(RadioFields.GENDER_GROUP)
  .drop()

usages.description: Probe visibility when the test needs to branch or defer assertions based on whether a radio group/control is shown in the current state.
usages.contextHint: Use when you need the boolean later in the flow (stored in Quest storage). For straightforward checks, prefer validateIsVisible(...).

items.summary: Validate that the selected radio value matches the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

  .drop()

usages.description: Validate the currently selected option for a radio group. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast. The RadioUiElement should represent the group whose selected value is being asserted.

usages.contextHint: Use when disabled state is a strict requirement (e.g., role/state-based restrictions). Prefer enum-backed RadioFields.* (RadioUiElement) over raw locators.

items.summary: Low-level insertion entrypoint that executes a radio selection using a raw locator + explicit ComponentType, passing optional insertion arguments.
