usages.contextHint: Prefer select(...) and enum-backed RadioUiElement in normal @Test code. This method is primarily for framework extensions/adapters/components where locators are computed at runtime.

items.summary: Core ROA quest object used in tests. Orchestrates ring activation (quest.use(...)), shared storage, and soft assertions for a single test run.

usages.description: Quest is injected into @Test methods and acts as the entry point for activating rings and running fluent test flows.
usages.contextHint: Prefer using the injected Quest parameter in tests. Quest owns the lifecycle of the thread-local SuperQuest context and soft-assert flushing via complete().

items.summary: Activate a ring (fluent service) for the current test and continue chaining UI/custom actions.

usages.code: quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
usages.description: Activate the UI ring for the current quest.
usages.contextHint: Use at the start of a chain or after drop() when switching rings. Prefer using project ring constants from Rings (e.g., Rings.RING_OF_UI) instead of raw UiServiceFluent.class.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_CUSTOM)
     .prepareSomething()
     .drop()
     .use(RING_OF_UI)
     .browser().navigate(getUiConfig().baseUrl())
usages.description: Switch rings mid-flow: use a custom ring for reusable logic, then drop back to Quest and activate another ring.
usages.contextHint: Use drop() on the current ring to return to Quest, then call use(...) to activate the next ring. Ring constants are typically defined in the project Rings class.

items.summary: Finish the quest: clears thread-local context and asserts all collected soft assertions. Always call at the end of a test flow.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
     .browser().navigate(getUiConfig().baseUrl())
     .complete()
usages.description: Finalize the fluent chain and fail the test if any collected soft assertions failed.
usages.contextHint: Call once at the end of the test chain. complete() is what flushes soft assertions and clears QuestHolder; without it soft failures may not be reported and thread-local context may leak.

items.summary: Result of executing a single Assertion. Captures whether the assertion passed and carries any diagnostic details produced by the validator (message/context) for reporting.

usages.code:
Assertion assertion = Assertion.builder()
    .target(UiTablesAssertionTarget.TABLE_VALUES)
    .type(TableAssertionTypes.NOT_CONTAINS_ROW)
    .expected("ERROR")
    .soft(false)
    .build();

quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
    .table().validate(Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS, assertion)
    .drop().complete();
usages.description: AssertionResult is produced internally when a validator evaluates an Assertion. Tests usually don’t handle AssertionResult directly; they rely on the validator + quest.complete() to surface failures and diagnostics.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: AssertionResult is emitted by the validation engine for reporting/logging. Test-side: prefer expressing validations via fluent services and assertions rather than consuming AssertionResult objects manually.

items.summary: Fluent service for list components (select/de-select items, read items/selection, and validate item visibility/enablement/selection). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides list() -> ListServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() using enum-backed ListUiElement registries (ListFields.*) instead of raw locators.

usages.description: Expose ListServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests enter the list chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list(). Then interact with list items by passing a ListUiElement that represents the list container (ListFields.*) plus the item value(s) as strings.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ListServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent; do not instantiate manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.list() (getListField()). Test-side: use ListFields.* (ListUiElement) to centralize the list locator/component type; pass item labels/keys as Strings according to the component’s selection strategy.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the given list item values are enabled (interactable) in the specified list.

usages.code:

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Button components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the button technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Button implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., ButtonComponentTypes) implementing ButtonComponentType to declare which button variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your ButtonUiElement field registry (e.g., ButtonFields) so each button locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Button implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define ButtonComponentType enums to represent the button technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from ButtonFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Button implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Table behavior across different UI technologies/variants (reading rows, projecting fields, filtering, sorting, and inserting cell/row values). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the TableComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType when custom table behavior is needed.

usages.description: Table defines the contract ROA uses to interact with tabular UI widgets in a type-safe way (row representation classes + TableField projections). Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior for reading/mapping rows, applying filters/sorts, and editing table cells/rows when supported.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: provide a dedicated implementation only when you need behavior beyond the default table handling (e.g., non-standard widgets). Bind custom implementations via @ImplementationOfType to a TableComponentType identifier. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Inserts values into a specific cell of a row (default cell index = 1) for the table mapped to the given row type and TableField.

usages.description: Used by fluent table/editing flows when a cell is editable and you want to insert one or more values into the cell resolved by TableField. The concrete implementation decides how to locate the row, resolve the cell for the given field, and perform input actions.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep TableField → column/cell resolution consistent with read/mapping logic, and make insertion idempotent where applicable (clear/replace vs append). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads a single row by index and maps only the specified TableField projections into the requested row type.

usages.description: Used when you need a partial row projection (only certain columns) instead of mapping an entire row model. Fluent table reads delegate to this for efficient, intention-revealing assertions and branching logic.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement projection mapping so TableField[] drives which cells are extracted and mapped (avoid hardcoded column indices). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads the first row matching the given search criteria and maps it to the specified row type.

usages.description: Used by fluent search/read flows when you want to locate a row by criteria (e.g., field values) and then map it into a row representation class. Implementations define how criteria are evaluated (visible text, normalized values, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep criteria evaluation consistent with TableField mapping and row text normalization (trimming, formatting). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads a range of rows (fromIndex..toIndex) from the table and maps them to the specified row type.

usages.description: Used by fluent table reads when you need a subset of rows (paging windows, range checks, sampling). The implementation controls how row indices map to DOM rows and how row models are built.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure row indexing rules are clear and consistent (1-based vs 0-based) across all read/insert APIs. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads the whole table while projecting only the specified TableField columns into the requested row type.

usages.description: Used when you want to map a table to a list of row models but only need a subset of columns. Fluent services use this to keep reads fast and intent-focused while still staying type-safe.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: treat TableField[] as the source of truth for projection and mapping (avoid fragile positional assumptions). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Inserts values into a specific cell of a row at the given cell index for the provided row type and TableField.

usages.description: Used for editable tables where a column can contain multiple editable targets (e.g., multiple inputs within a cell). Fluent editing flows delegate to this when explicit cell index selection is required.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define how cellIndex maps to editable targets within a cell and keep it consistent across widgets. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Finds the first row matching the search criteria and inserts values into the specified cell (by TableField + cell index).

usages.description: Used when you want to edit a row identified by criteria (not by index) and update a specific cell/target. Fluent flows typically use this for “find row → edit cell” operations in data grids.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep criteria matching robust and deterministic (avoid ambiguous matches; consider normalization). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads a single row by index and maps it to the specified row representation type.

usages.description: Used by fluent reads when you want the entire row mapped into a row model (row representation class). Implementations define how row models are populated from table cells.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep row indexing consistent and ensure row mapping aligns with your row representation class design (constructors/setters/field mapping). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads a range of rows (fromIndex..toIndex) while projecting only the specified TableField columns into the requested row type.

usages.description: Used when you need a window of rows but only certain columns matter. Fluent reads delegate here to keep table reads efficient and stable by avoiding unnecessary parsing.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure projection mapping is consistent across readRow/readTable overloads and respects TableField ordering. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads the first row matching the search criteria and maps only the specified TableField projections into the requested row type.

usages.description: Used for “find row by criteria → read projected fields” flows. Fluent services use this to assert or branch based on specific row content without mapping the entire row.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep criteria matching deterministic and make projection mapping consistent with other read overloads. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Inserts (maps) an entire row object into the table at the specified row index.

usages.description: Used for table editing flows where a row representation object is used as the source of values. The implementation decides how row objects map to editable cells (TableField mapping, column resolution, per-cell editors).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define a clear mapping strategy from rowObject → editable cells (field-to-column mapping) and keep it consistent with readRow mapping. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Finds the first row matching the search criteria and inserts values into the specified cell (default cell index = 1) identified by TableField.

usages.description: Used for “find row → edit cell” flows when you don’t need to specify a cell index. Fluent services delegate here for common table widgets where each column maps to a single editable target per row.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep matching and cell resolution consistent with other insertCellValue overloads to avoid editing the wrong row/cell. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Sorts the table by the specified TableField column using the provided SortingStrategy.

usages.description: Used by fluent table flows when sorting by a column is required. Implementations must align SortingStrategy with the table widget’s sorting controls and ensure stable state transitions (e.g., cycling asc/desc/none).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement SortingStrategy in a deterministic way (avoid accidental double toggles) and scope sorting to the intended table instance. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Finds the first row matching the search criteria and inserts (maps) an entire row object into that row.

usages.description: Used for “find row by criteria → update row using object” flows. Fluent services can delegate to this when editing a row should be driven by a row representation object rather than individual cell values.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep criteria matching deterministic and rowObject→cell mapping consistent with readRow mapping rules. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Applies FilterStrategy to a table column (TableField) using the provided values.

usages.description: Used by fluent table flows to filter rows by column. Implementations must align FilterStrategy semantics with the table widget’s filtering behavior (typing, apply triggers, debounce, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement FilterStrategy deterministically and ensure filter controls are scoped to the correct table instance/column. Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Reads the entire table and maps it to a list of the specified row type.

usages.description: Used by fluent reads when you want to map the entire visible table into row models for assertions, comparisons, or further filtering in code. Implementations should make row indexing and mapping stable and predictable.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep row indexing consistent across the API and ensure mapping is stable with dynamic tables (pagination, lazy loading). Test-side: prefer TableServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).table() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Radio components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the radio technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Radio implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., RadioComponentTypes) implementing RadioComponentType to declare which radio variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your RadioUiElement field registry (e.g., RadioFields) so each radio locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Radio implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define RadioComponentType enums to represent the radio technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from RadioFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Radio implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Fluent service for alert/toast components (read alert text/value, visibility checks and validations). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides alert() -> AlertServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert() using enum-backed AlertUiElement registries (AlertFields.*) instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Expose AlertServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests enter the alert chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).alert(). Then validate or read alert messages using enum-backed AlertUiElement constants (AlertFields.*), finishing with drop() and complete().
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: AlertServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent and uses the configured alert component implementation; do not instantiate it manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.alert() (delegating to getAlertField()). Test-side: prefer AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) to keep alert locators/component typing (and optional hooks) centralized.

items.summary: Validate that the alert is hidden (not visible), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateIsHidden(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate that an alert is not visible. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion. Useful for flows where a message should disappear or should not be shown for a given state/role.
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) for the alert locator. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the alert is visible.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateIsVisible(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that an alert is shown (useful to confirm user feedback after an action such as save/submit).
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) to centralize the selector. If the alert is expected to appear after async work, pair with loader waits or your project’s synchronization utilities.

items.summary: Evaluate whether the alert is visible and store the boolean result in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .isVisible(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Probe alert visibility when the test needs to branch (e.g., optional toast appears only in some scenarios) or when you want to defer assertions to a later step.
usages.contextHint: Result is stored in Quest storage. For straightforward checks, prefer validateIsVisible(...). Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) rather than raw locators.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the alert is hidden (not visible).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateIsHidden(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that an alert is not displayed (useful for negative checks or to ensure the UI is clear before proceeding).
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) to centralize the selector and keep tests free of raw locators.

items.summary: Validate that the alert text/value matches the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateValue(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT, "Saved successfully", true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate an alert message. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion. Useful when verifying multiple messages/states in one test flow.
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) for the alert locator/component type. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the alert text/value matches the expected value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateValue(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT, "Saved successfully")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion that the alert message matches the expected content (common for success/error feedback).
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) to centralize the selector. If messages vary by locale/environment, keep expected text in test data rather than duplicating literals.

items.summary: Read the current alert text/value and store it in Quest storage.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .getValue(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Capture an alert message for later comparison (e.g., assert on a different step, compare across screens, or use it in conditional logic).
usages.contextHint: Retrieved value is stored in Quest storage. Prefer validateValue(...) when you want an immediate assertion. Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) rather than raw locators.

items.summary: Validate that the alert is visible, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .alert()
  .validateIsVisible(AlertFields.GLOBAL_ALERT, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate that an alert is shown. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement) for the alert locator. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for ItemList behavior across different UI technologies/variants (select/deselect items, discover items, and verify enabled/visible/selected state). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the ItemListComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: ItemList defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with list-style components (menus, navigation lists, selectable lists, etc.). Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported list technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding ItemListComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct ItemList implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are enabled within the provided container.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations to confirm specific list items are interactive before attempting selection. Implementations should align enabled semantics with the list technology (disabled attribute, aria-disabled, click-blocking wrappers, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define enabled semantics consistently and keep item resolution scoped to the container. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects item(s) within the container located by locator using the provided Strategy, returning the affected item text when applicable.

usages.description: Strategy-based deselection supports dynamic flows (e.g., deselect the first currently selected item). Implementations should define how list item selection is represented (active class, aria-selected, checkbox-style lists, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are selected within the provided container.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations to assert list selection state after selection/deselection or to verify default active items. Implementations must keep selected detection consistent with select/deSelect behavior.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selected-state detection aligned with the list technology (active class, aria-selected, checked inputs, etc.). Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are visible within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-scoped visibility checks are used when the service layer can reliably locate the list container by selector and needs to assert item presence/visibility by text.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep lookups relative to the located container and normalize text matching. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are selected at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global selected checks are useful when list scope is unambiguous on the page. Fluent validations often prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple lists exist.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped logic on pages with multiple lists. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more items (by text) within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-scoped selection is used when the list container is identified by a stable selector and items are selected by readable text. Implementations should keep selection idempotent (click only when not already selected).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep item resolution scoped to the container and align selected detection with list technology semantics. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more items (by text) within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped selection is the common approach on pages with multiple lists. Fluent list flows typically delegate to this method when selecting specific item labels within a scoped UI region.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: make selection idempotent and keep lookups relative to the container to avoid selecting the wrong list instance. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether items identified by one or more locators are visible.

usages.description: Locator-based visibility checks are used when items are best modeled by stable selectors (IDs, data-testid, etc.) rather than text. Fluent validations may delegate to this overload in advanced scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators target the intended item elements and consider animation/timing in visibility checks. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more items (by text) within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped deselection is used to clean up state within a specific list region (navigation tabs, selectable lists, etc.). Fluent services delegate to this method when deselecting explicit item labels.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep deselection idempotent (only click when selected) and scoped to the container. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more items (by text) within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-scoped deselection is used when the list container is identified by a stable selector and the flow needs to deselect items by readable text within that container.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator-based overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to centralize selection-state logic. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns all item texts within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Used for discovery/coverage checks and dynamic flows where item labels must be enumerated. Locator-based overloads typically delegate to the SmartWebElement overload to centralize text extraction logic.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to avoid duplicated enumeration logic. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects item(s) within the container located by locator using the provided Strategy, returning the chosen item text when applicable.

usages.description: Strategy-based selection supports dynamic flows (e.g., select first/last/random item). Fluent services use this when the exact item is not predetermined and the list needs to be driven by Strategy.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether items identified by one or more locators are enabled.

usages.description: Locator-based enabled checks are used when items are best modeled by stable selectors rather than text. Fluent validations can delegate to this overload in advanced scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators target the intended clickable/selectable elements and apply enabled semantics consistently. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns all item texts within the provided container.

usages.description: Used for discovery and coverage checks (what items exist) and as a foundation for Strategy-based selection flows. Fluent services may expose this for assertions and diagnostics.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep item enumeration aligned with the list technology (items, options, tabs) and normalize extracted text. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects one or more items (by text) at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global deselection is useful when list scope is unambiguous. Fluent services often prefer container-scoped overloads on complex pages with multiple lists.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped logic when multiple lists exist. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are enabled within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-scoped enabled checks are used when the list container is identified by a stable selector and items are verified by text. Delegating to the SmartWebElement overload keeps resolution rules consistent.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to avoid duplicated lookup logic. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are visible within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped visibility checks are used when multiple lists exist and visibility must be asserted within a specific region (menus, cards, modal lists, tabs, etc.).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep lookups relative to the container and normalize text matching for stable assertions. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects item(s) in the provided container using the provided Strategy, returning the chosen item text when applicable.

usages.description: Strategy-based deselection supports dynamic flows (e.g., deselect the first selected item). Fluent services use this when a specific item is not predetermined and cleanup/branching depends on Strategy.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of selected item texts within the provided container.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations and branching flows to determine which items are currently selected inside a specific list region (tabs, nav lists, selectable lists).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure selected detection matches your list technology (active class, aria-selected, checked inputs, etc.) and normalize extracted text. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Returns the list of selected item texts within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based selected retrieval is used when the service layer has a stable container selector. Delegating to the SmartWebElement overload centralizes selection detection logic.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to the SmartWebElement overloads to avoid duplicated selection-detection logic. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deselects items identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based deselection is useful when items are best modeled by stable selectors rather than text. Fluent services can use this overload in advanced cases where label matching is not appropriate.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators point to the selectable items and keep deselection idempotent. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects item(s) in the provided container using the provided Strategy, returning the chosen item text when applicable.

usages.description: Strategy-based selection supports dynamic flows where a specific item is not predetermined (first/last/random/all). Fluent services use this for exploratory scenarios and generic setup steps.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement Strategy handling using Strategy.FIRST/Strategy.LAST/Strategy.RANDOM/Strategy.ALL (no helper methods). Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are visible at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global visibility checks are useful when list scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations often prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple lists exist on the page.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global text matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped logic on complex pages. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are selected within the container located by locator.

usages.description: Locator-scoped selected checks are used when the list container is identified by a stable selector and selected state must be verified by item text inside that container.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: delegate locator overloads to container-based logic to keep selected detection consistent. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects items identified by one or more locators.

usages.description: Locator-based selection is used when stable selectors exist per item and label matching is not desired. Fluent services may delegate to this overload in advanced scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selection idempotent and ensure locators point to the selectable items. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether items identified by one or more locators are selected.

usages.description: Locator-based selected checks are used when stable selectors exist per item or when labels are unreliable (duplicates/localization). Fluent validations can delegate to this overload when needed.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locators target the correct selectable items and keep selected semantics consistent with select/deSelect. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Selects one or more items (by text) at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global selection is useful when list scope is unambiguous on the page. Fluent services often prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple lists exist to avoid selecting an item from the wrong list.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global text matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped selection on complex pages. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the specified items (by text) are enabled at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global enabled checks are useful when list scope is unambiguous. Fluent validations often prefer container-scoped overloads when multiple lists exist or when the same text appears in different regions.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: use global matching only when safe; prefer container-scoped checks on pages with multiple lists. Test-side: prefer ListServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).list() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for generic, cross-cutting UI validations that don’t belong to a specific component service (e.g., “text is present in a given HTML field/tag”), supporting both fail-fast and aggregated (soft) assertion styles. This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides validate()/validation() -> ValidationServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).validate().

usages.description: Expose ValidationServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests can run generic validations via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).validate(). Use it for broad page/text presence checks when a component-specific fluent method (input/button/alert/etc.) is not the right fit.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ValidationServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent; do not instantiate it manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.validate() (delegating to getValidation()). Prefer component-specific validations when you’re asserting a specific UI element; use this service for page-level “text exists within field/tag” style checks.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the expected text is present within the specified HTML field/tag on the current page.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .validate()
  .validateTextInField(javax.swing.text.html.HTML.Tag.BODY, "Account created")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast assertion for page-level text presence inside a specific HTML tag (commonly BODY). Useful for verifying navigation outcomes, success banners rendered as plain text, or global page content when a dedicated element locator is not required.
usages.contextHint: Use when the text must be present to proceed. If you want aggregated checks across multiple texts/sections, use the boolean overload.

items.summary: Validate that the expected text is present within the specified HTML field/tag, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .validate()
  .validateTextInField(javax.swing.text.html.HTML.Tag.BODY, "Welcome", true)
  .validateTextInField(javax.swing.text.html.HTML.Tag.BODY, "Transfer funds", true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate multiple page-level text expectations in one flow. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use soft=true when validating multiple UI conditions in one run. Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast. BODY is the typical tag for whole-page checks; use more specific tags only when needed.

items.summary: Marker interface describing how a value is validated (comparison/match semantics). Implemented by enums that define domain-specific assertion types (e.g., table assertions via TableAssertionTypes).

usages.description: Use an AssertionType enum to declare the validation rule (e.g., contains row / not contains row for tables). The validator executes the correct comparison logic based on the enum constant.
usages.contextHint: Choose an AssertionType that matches the AssertionTarget domain you’re validating (table types for table targets). Keep expected values aligned with what the validator extracts (row strings, collections, element states, etc.).

items.summary: Active execution context used by project-side helpers (data creators, preconditions, rippers, hooks). You can obtain it via QuestHolder.get() from anywhere during a running test thread. SuperQuest provides access to shared storage/soft assertions and also exposes getOriginal() to reach the underlying Quest chain when needed.

usages.description: Get the active SuperQuest for the current test thread and access its Storage. This is the typical entry point for project-side helper code where the Quest parameter is not available.
usages.contextHint: Prefer the injected Quest parameter in test methods. QuestHolder.get() is intended for helper code that runs during test execution on the same thread (data creators, preconditions, cleanup functions).

usages.description: Access the underlying Quest instance via getOriginal() when helper code needs to switch back into the raw Quest chain (e.g., to activate a ring from a utility that only has SuperQuest).
usages.contextHint: Usually you don’t need this directly. Tests should use the Quest parameter; rings use drop(); helpers use SuperQuest mainly for storage access. Use getOriginal() only when a helper must explicitly drive ring execution.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Input components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the input technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Input implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., InputComponentTypes) implementing InputComponentType to declare which input variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your InputUiElement field registry (e.g., InputFields) so each input locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Input implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define InputComponentType enums to represent the input technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from InputFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Input implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Attach a StaticDataProvider to a test (or class) so its staticTestData() map is loaded into the Quest storage before the test runs.

items.summary: Marks a class as a ROA Ring: a Spring-managed test service (@Service) with prototype scope and lazy initialization. Rings are activated from tests via quest.use(RING_CLASS) to expose a fluent domain/service API (e.g., UI, API, or custom business rings).

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Accordion components. items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Toggle behavior across different UI technologies/variants (activate/deactivate and verify enabled/activated state). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the ToggleComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Toggle defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with toggle-style controls (switches, on/off controls, yes/no toggles). Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported toggle technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding ToggleComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Toggle implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Checks whether the toggle identified by locator is enabled.

usages.description: Locator-based enabled checks are used by fluent validations when a toggle is modeled by a stable selector rather than label matching. Implementations should align enabled semantics with the toggle technology (disabled attribute, aria-disabled, wrapper behavior).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: define enabled semantics consistently (disabled, aria-disabled, click-blocking wrappers). Test-side: prefer ToggleServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).toggle() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the toggle identified by locator is activated.

usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement activation detection using the toggle’s real state signal (checked input, aria-checked, active class) and keep it consistent with activate/deactivate behavior. Test-side: prefer ToggleServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).toggle() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Deactivates the toggle identified by label within the provided container.

usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep label resolution relative to the container and make deactivation idempotent (only click when currently activated). Test-side: prefer ToggleServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).toggle() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring list elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s ListService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., ListFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed lists instead of raw Selenium locators.

items.summary: Fluent service for accordion components (expand/collapse items, read title/text, and validate expanded/collapsed/enabled/disabled states). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides accordion() -> AccordionServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion() using enum-backed AccordionUiElement registries (AccordionFields.*).

usages.description: Expose AccordionServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests enter the accordion chain via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).accordion(). Then interact with accordion components using an enum-backed AccordionUiElement (AccordionFields.*) plus item identifiers (usually item titles/keys as Strings), finishing with drop() and complete().

Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.accordion() (delegating to getAccordionField()). Test-side: prefer AccordionFields.* (AccordionUiElement) to keep locators/component typing (and optional hooks) centralized; pass item titles/keys as Strings as defined by your accordion component implementation.

items.summary: Framework-provided Spring service container exposed only to BaseQuestSequential.beforeAll/afterAll. Use it to fetch underlying Spring services from a Ring (e.g., a low-level UI driver/session service from UiServiceFluent) when you need low-level access in class-level setup/cleanup.
