items.summary: Framework storage namespaces used during a test run. Use these keys when you need to read/write data in specific lifecycle areas (test arguments, precondition arguments, static data, hook data).

usages.description: Read test-argument data (values produced/registered for the current test run). Commonly used by project-side utilities when building context-aware models or reusing previously created artifacts.
usages.contextHint: Use in project-side code (data creators, utilities, custom rings) when you want to reuse values already available during the test body. ARGUMENTS typically corresponds to data available during the test execution.

usages.description: Read precondition argument data created during @Journey execution (pre-test setup). Useful when a later precondition/utility depends on what was prepared before the test body runs.
usages.contextHint: Use when your setup flow (@Journey / Preconditions) stores inputs or produced models for later reuse. PRE_ARGUMENTS is typically populated by pre-test journeys.

usages.description: Read the raw STATIC_DATA map that was loaded via @StaticTestData and access values directly by string key.
usages.contextHint: Prefer DataExtractorsTest.staticTestData(key) + retrieve(...) for static data access. Use direct map access only in rare project-side utilities that need raw map operations.

usages.description: Read hook-scoped values written by hooks into the HOOKS map and access them via a key object (often an enum).
usages.contextHint: Prefer BaseQuest.hookData(key, type) in tests. Use direct HOOKS map access mainly inside framework/project utilities where BaseQuest is not available.

items.summary: Model field annotation that maps a data property to a concrete UI element for insertion flows. It declares which locator registry to use (locatorClass), which element constant within it (elementEnum), and the insertion order used by the insertion engine.

usages.description: Annotate model fields with @InsertionElement so ROA’s insertion engine can reflect over the object and insert each value into the right UI element. locatorClass points to the enum/registry that defines UI element locators, elementEnum selects the specific constant, and order controls the deterministic insertion sequence.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: use on data model fields that are meant to be inserted via insertion().insertData(model). Ensure elementEnum matches the constant name in the locatorClass enum. Test-side: prefer crafting the model via @Craft and inserting via insertion().insertData(...) rather than manually filling each field.

items.summary: Specifies the locator registry class (typically an enum like InputFields) that contains the UI element constant referenced by elementEnum.

usages.code:
@InsertionElement(locatorClass = InputFields.class, elementEnum = "LOCATION_FIELD", order = 1)
private String location;
usages.description: Point to the enum/class that declares the UI element locators used by the insertion engine.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: locatorClass should be a UI element registry (commonly an enum implementing the appropriate UiElement contract). The insertion engine uses it together with elementEnum to resolve the target UI element.

items.summary: Specifies the enum constant name (as a string) within locatorClass that identifies the exact UI element to target for insertion.

usages.code:
@InsertionElement(locatorClass = InputFields.class, elementEnum = "CUSTOMER_NAME_FIELD", order = 2)
private String customerName;
usages.description: Select the specific UI element constant inside the locator registry so the insertion engine can locate and populate the corresponding field.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: elementEnum must match the constant name exactly as declared in locatorClass. Use stable, intention-revealing constant names to keep mappings readable.

items.summary: Controls the deterministic processing order for insertion; lower numbers are inserted first.

usages.description: Use order to guarantee a predictable insertion sequence when the UI requires fields to be set in a specific order (e.g., selecting location before enabling dependent fields).
usages.contextHint: Project-side: set order only when sequencing matters; otherwise you can still use explicit values to keep behavior deterministic across refactors. The insertion engine processes fields from lowest to highest order.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Tab components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the tab technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Tab implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., TabComponentTypes) implementing TabComponentType to declare which tab variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your TabUiElement field registry (e.g., TabFields) so each tab locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Tab implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define TabComponentType enums to represent the tab technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from TabFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Tab implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Project-side contract for declaring link elements (locators + component type + optional before/after hooks) that integrate with ROA’s LinkService. Implement this as an enum (e.g., LinkFields) so UI actions can target strongly-typed links instead of raw Selenium locators.

usages.description: Define a project enum that implements LinkUiElement to centralize link locators and map each link to a concrete LinkComponentType. The before/after hooks are optional—use them only when a specific link needs extra stabilization before clicking or validating state.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: keep link definitions in a single enum (e.g., LinkFields) and map each constant to the correct LinkComponentType so the framework selects the right link implementation. Add a before/after hook only for dynamic links; otherwise rely on the framework’s standard synchronization. Test-side usage is via fluent LinkService methods, which are documented under those method IDs.

items.summary: Enum used by hook annotations (e.g., @DbHook.when) to control whether a hook runs once before all tests in a class or once after all tests in a class.

usages.description: Run the hook flow once before any @Test methods in the class (setup phase).
usages.contextHint: Use BEFORE for environment initialization (DB seed/warmup), shared setup, or storing hook outputs for tests to read via hookData(...).

usages.description: Run the hook flow once after all @Test methods in the class finish (teardown phase).
usages.contextHint: Use AFTER for one-time class cleanup (clearing seeded data, closing shared resources). If cleanup must run per test, prefer @Ripper instead.

items.summary: Thread-local access to the active SuperQuest for the current test thread. Use it only in places where the Quest parameter is not available (e.g., DataCreator, Preconditions, DataCleaner, custom rings/helpers).

usages.description: Get the active SuperQuest and read data from storage when you're outside the test method (no Quest parameter available).
usages.contextHint: Typical in project-side utilities (DataCreator / Preconditions / DataCleaner / custom rings). Avoid using QuestHolder in test methods—prefer the injected Quest parameter.

items.summary: Remove the SuperQuest from the current thread to avoid leaking test context between tests.

usages.code: quest.complete();
usages.description: Normally you never call QuestHolder.clear() directly—Quest.complete() does it for you.
usages.contextHint: Call complete() at the end of every test flow. Only clear() manually in very rare custom runner/extension scenarios.

items.summary: Bind a SuperQuest to the current thread (framework/extension responsibility).

usages.code: QuestHolder.set(superQuest);
usages.description: Framework/extension usage: binds the current SuperQuest during test startup so rings and helpers can access it via QuestHolder.get().
usages.contextHint: Do not call from normal test or project code. This is handled by the ROA engine (e.g., QuestFactory/Oracle/decorators) before the test body runs.

items.summary: Return the SuperQuest bound to the current thread (or null if not initialized).

usages.description: Access storage/artifacts from helper code that runs during a test thread but doesn’t receive Quest as a parameter.
usages.contextHint: Use inside project-side factories/services (e.g., DataCreator, Preconditions, cleanup functions). If QuestHolder.get() is null, you're calling it outside the ROA-managed test execution.

items.summary: Static factory for DataExtractor instances used in UI flows. Provides helpers for extracting stored table rows and for reading values from captured/intercepted responses using JSONPath, so tests can call retrieve(extractor, type) without dealing with storage internals.

usages.description: Use DataExtractorsUi helpers to build DataExtractor instances for values produced and stored during UI flows (e.g., table read results or response captures), then retrieve typed objects via BaseQuest.retrieve(...).
usages.contextHint: Prefer DataExtractorsUi for UI-focused extraction patterns instead of building custom DataExtractorImpl in each test. These extractors are meant to be used after the producing flow has stored the relevant artifacts in quest storage.

items.summary: Creates a DataExtractor that finds the first stored table row whose values include all provided indicators (case-insensitive) for the given table key.

usages.description: Use when you stored a table read result and need to locate a specific row by one or more “contains” indicators (useful for quick lookups without writing explicit criteria objects).
usages.contextHint: Works best when the indicator strings uniquely identify a row in the table. Use the table enum key that was used for the table read/store operation.

items.summary: Creates a DataExtractor that reads a value from the most recent stored/captured response matching the provided endpoint substring, using a JSONPath expression.

usages.description: Use when a UI flow captured/stored a response for a given endpoint substring (e.g., via request interception) and you want to extract a field using JSONPath from the latest matching response.
usages.contextHint: Call this after the interception/capture has happened so the response is present in storage. Keep JSONPath expressions stable by centralizing them if they are reused across tests.

items.summary: Creates a DataExtractor that returns the stored table row at the given index for the specified table key.

usages.description: Use when you want a specific row by index from a previously stored table read result (e.g., first row after sorting/filtering).
usages.contextHint: Use after a table read flow stored rows for that table key. Ensure your test’s index aligns with how rows are stored/numbered by the table service.

items.summary: Creates a DataExtractor that reads a value from the Nth most recent stored/captured response matching the provided endpoint substring, using a JSONPath expression.

usages.description: Use when the same endpoint was captured multiple times in the same test flow and you need to read from a specific historical response (e.g., compare values before/after an update).
usages.contextHint: Index is typically 1-based from the end: 1 = latest, 2 = previous. Requires that the endpoint was captured at least as many times as the requested index.

items.summary: Fluent service for asserting network interceptions captured during UI flows (e.g., validating HTTP response status patterns for requests matching a URL substring). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and provides interceptor() -> InterceptorServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).interceptor() after actions that trigger network calls.

usages.description: Expose InterceptorServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests can validate intercepted responses via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).interceptor(). Use it to assert that UI-triggered network calls (matched by URL substring) returned the expected HTTP status class/prefix (e.g., 2xx success, 4xx client error).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: interception is configured via the UI adapter (e.g., request interception annotation/extension) and stored in Quest storage; do not instantiate the service manually. Use this service after the UI action that triggers the request(s), and choose a URL substring that uniquely matches the intended endpoint(s).

items.summary: Validate that all intercepted responses whose request URL contains the given substring have an HTTP status in the specified status class (prefix), with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .interceptor()
  .validateResponseHaveStatus("/api/payments", 2, true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate the status class for all matching intercepted responses. The statusPrefix is the hundreds digit (e.g., 2 for 2xx, 4 for 4xx, 5 for 5xx). When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Pick a URL substring that targets the intended calls (avoid overly broad substrings). Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Hard-assert that all intercepted responses whose request URL contains the given substring have an HTTP status in the specified status class (prefix).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .interceptor()
  .validateResponseHaveStatus("/api/payments", 2)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast validation that all matching intercepted calls succeeded (e.g., statusPrefix=2 means every matched call returned 2xx). Useful for asserting that a UI flow triggered a successful backend call without asserting the full body.
usages.contextHint: The statusPrefix is the hundreds digit (2/3/4/5). Use this after the UI action that triggers the request(s), and ensure interception is enabled/configured for the test (via the UI adapter).

items.summary: Test-side precondition annotation. Runs before the test body and executes a project-defined precondition referenced by a string key (typically coming from a project enum implementing PreQuestJourney). Optionally passes input models via @JourneyData(...) (keys typically coming from a project enum implementing DataForge). Can be repeated multiple times on the same test (execution order controlled by order; defaults to 0).

usages.description: Single journey with input data: the framework runs one precondition before the test body and provides crafted input model(s) to that precondition. In real projects, value is usually written as a constant from an enum that implements PreQuestJourney (e.g., Preconditions implements PreQuestJourney<Preconditions>), and journeyData values are usually written as constants from an enum that implements DataForge (e.g., DataCreator implements DataForge<DataCreator>).
usages.contextHint: Use when your precondition needs input models (e.g., create something using crafted data). order is optional and can be omitted for a single @Journey; specify it when multiple @Journey annotations exist.

usages.description: Single journey without input data: the framework runs one precondition before the test body when no crafted models are required. In real projects, value is typically written as a constant from an enum that implements PreQuestJourney (e.g., Preconditions implements PreQuestJourney<Preconditions>) so the precondition registry stays strongly-typed and discoverable.
usages.contextHint: Use when setup can run without any crafted inputs (e.g., login, navigation, environment reset). order is optional and can be omitted for a single @Journey.

usages.description: Multiple journeys: stack @Journey annotations to run multiple preconditions before the test body. order controls execution order (lower runs first). In real projects, values are typically constants from a PreQuestJourney enum (e.g., Preconditions implements PreQuestJourney<Preconditions>), and any JourneyData entries are typically constants from a DataForge enum (e.g., DataCreator implements DataForge<DataCreator>).
usages.contextHint: Prefer explicit order values whenever more than one @Journey is present so setup executes deterministically. order is optional (defaults to 0), but should be set when multiple journeys exist. Use journeyData only on the journeys that require crafted inputs.

items.summary: Marker interface for assertion targets (where an assertion is applied). Implemented by enums that define a concrete target domain (e.g., UI tables via UiTablesAssertionTarget).

usages.description: Use an AssertionTarget enum to declare what the validator should assert against (e.g., table row values vs table element collection). The validator interprets the target to select the correct extraction/validation path.
usages.contextHint: Targets are domain-specific: UI table assertions commonly use UiTablesAssertionTarget. Pair the target with a compatible AssertionType (e.g., TableAssertionTypes) for meaningful validations.

items.summary: Marker contract for classifying Select components. Project-side enums implement this interface to declare the select technologies/variants (e.g., Bootstrap), and ROA uses that type to resolve the matching Select implementation annotated with @ImplementationOfType at runtime.

usages.description: Create a project enum (e.g., SelectComponentTypes) implementing SelectComponentType to declare which select variants exist in your UI. Reference that enum from your SelectUiElement field registry (e.g., SelectFields) so each select locator is tagged with the correct type. ROA then matches that type to the actual component implementation via @ImplementationOfType, ensuring the framework instantiates the right Select implementation for the element under test.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: define SelectComponentType enums to represent the select technologies/variants your UI exposes, and reuse those values from SelectFields (or your equivalent field enum) so each locator is explicitly mapped to a type. Framework-side: each concrete Select implementation must be annotated with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the enum constant (often mirrored via a shared string constant), otherwise ROA can’t reliably resolve the correct component implementation.

items.summary: Project-side contract for post-test cleanup (teardown). Implement a project enum (e.g., DataCleaner) that maps cleanup targets to a Consumer<SuperQuest>, then trigger it from tests via @Ripper(targets = { "<KEY>" }).

usages.description: Define a project-side cleanup enum (DataRipper implementation) using a lambda Consumer<SuperQuest>, then reference the cleanup target from @Ripper on the test method so teardown runs automatically after the test finishes.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: DataCleaner is created/maintained in the test project and represents your cleanup registry. Test-side: @Ripper runs after the test finishes; keep cleanup logic deterministic and safe to run even when the test fails mid-flow.

items.summary: Static factory for DataExtractor instances that read values from the STATIC_DATA map in quest storage (typically loaded via @StaticTestData). Keys are string constants defined by a project StaticDataProvider (e.g., StaticData.ORDER).

usages.description: Load project static test data into storage via @StaticTestData, then extract typed values by key using DataExtractorsTest.staticTestData(key) + retrieve(...).
usages.contextHint: Used inside tests after @StaticTestData has executed (method-level or class-level). Keys should come from your project StaticDataProvider constants (e.g., StaticData.USERNAME) rather than hardcoded strings.

items.summary: Creates a DataExtractor that reads the STATIC_DATA map from storage and returns the value for the given string key.

usages.description: Extract a single value from the static test data map by key and use it in the test flow.
usages.contextHint: Pairs with @StaticTestData. The key must be a string constant defined by a project class implementing StaticDataProvider (e.g., StaticData.ORDER), so static test data keys stay centralized and consistent.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Button behavior across different UI technologies/variants. Concrete implementations (e.g., Bootstrap) implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the ButtonComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Button is the contract that defines how ROA interacts with button-like elements (clicking and state checks). Technology-specific implementations provide the actual Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported button technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding ButtonComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Button implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Clicks the interactive element inside a table cell/container (e.g., link/button/icon) using the component’s technology-specific rules.

usages.description: This component-level operation is typically used by higher-level services when a button-like control is embedded inside table cells or grid containers. The concrete Button implementation decides what is considered clickable for the given UI technology and performs the click accordingly.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this in your Button technology/variant class. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks a button identified by its visible text at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Defines the component contract for clicking a button by its label when no explicit container is provided. Services typically use this when the button is uniquely identifiable by its text on the current page.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement text-based lookup in a robust way for your technology (buttons, role='button', etc.). Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks a button identified by its visible text within the provided container element.

usages.description: Defines a container-scoped click by label. This is useful for repeated UI patterns (cards, rows, modals) where the same button label may exist multiple times on the page and must be resolved relative to a specific container.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep the lookup relative (use ".//" selectors) so resolution stays inside the container. Test-side: this is typically exercised via fluent services that first locate the container and then delegate to the resolved Button implementation. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether a button with the given text is enabled within the provided container.

usages.description: Defines a container-scoped enabled check by label. Services use this to validate actionable state in repeated sections (tables/cards/modals) without relying on global uniqueness.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement enabled checks consistent with your technology (native disabled attribute, aria-disabled, CSS state, etc.). Test-side: surfaced via fluent validations (e.g., validateIsEnabled/validateIsDisabled) that delegate to this component contract. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the first button-like element resolved inside the given container is visible.

usages.description: Defines a container-based visibility check when the service/flow already narrowed the scope and expects a single primary button within that container.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep selector rules aligned with what your UI considers a button. Test-side: typically used indirectly through fluent validations that work with resolved UI elements/containers. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the button identified by the given locator is enabled.

usages.description: Defines an enabled check for locator-driven button resolution. Services use this form when the target button is best represented by a stable locator rather than text matching.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locator resolution respects the component’s scoping rules (root/container) used in your BaseComponent. Test-side: the fluent layer usually owns the locator/element definitions and delegates to this method through the resolved Button implementation. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether a button with the given text is visible within the provided container.

usages.description: Defines a container-scoped visibility check by label. This is useful when multiple repeated containers exist and visibility must be asserted relative to a specific container instance.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep the lookup relative to the container to avoid accidentally matching a different instance elsewhere on the page. Test-side: typically surfaced through fluent assertions/validations. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks the first button-like element resolved inside the provided container.

usages.description: Defines a container-based click when the flow already narrowed the scope (e.g., click the primary action inside a card/row/modal section).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: choose selectors consistent with the technology and component semantics. Test-side: typically executed via fluent services that resolve the target container/UI element first. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the first button-like element resolved inside the provided container is enabled.

usages.description: Defines a container-based enabled check when a single primary button is expected inside the container scope.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure enabled state matches your UI’s accessibility/state model. Test-side: typically reached through fluent validations that delegate to this component method. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether a button with the given text is visible at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Defines a visibility check by label when the button is expected to be uniquely identifiable by text across the current page scope.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: make text matching resilient (normalize-space, support role-based buttons if applicable). Test-side: typically surfaced through fluent validateIsVisible/validateIsHidden calls. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether the button identified by the given locator is visible.

usages.description: Defines a locator-driven visibility check. This is used when a button is best modeled by a stable locator rather than text matching.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure locator resolution aligns with how your components scope and find elements. Test-side: typically executed via fluent validations on a resolved ButtonUiElement. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks the button identified by the given locator.

usages.description: Defines a locator-driven click. Services use this when a button has a reliable selector and does not require text matching or container scoping.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: consider click stability concerns (scroll into view, wait for clickable, etc.) in the concrete implementation. Test-side: usually invoked through fluent click operations on resolved button elements. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether a button with the given text is enabled at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Defines an enabled check by label when the button is expected to be uniquely identifiable by text in the current page scope.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement enabled semantics consistent with your UI technology (native disabled, aria-disabled, etc.). Test-side: typically surfaced through fluent validateIsEnabled/validateIsDisabled flows. Test-side: prefer ButtonServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).button() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Fluent service for browser-level navigation and context switching (navigate to URL, back/forward/refresh, tab/window handling, frame switching, and native browser alert handling). This service is part of the UI ring and is typically exposed through your project’s UiServiceFluent facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService> and exposes browser()/navigation() -> NavigationServiceFluent) so tests can call quest.use(RING_OF_UI).browser() as the entrypoint for navigation flows.

usages.description: Expose NavigationServiceFluent from your UiServiceFluent facade (AppUiService) so tests can drive browser navigation and switch context (tabs/windows/frames) via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).browser(). This keeps WebDriver navigation calls inside the fluent ring and consistent with the rest of the framework chaining style.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: NavigationServiceFluent is wired by UiServiceFluent; do not instantiate it manually. Project-side: expose it via AppUiService.browser() (delegating to getNavigation()). Use it for browser-level operations; use component services (button/link/input/etc.) for element interactions.

items.summary: Validate that the current native browser alert text equals the expected value, with optional soft-assert behavior controlled by the boolean flag.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .validateAlertText("Are you sure?", true)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Validate alert text when a native JavaScript alert/confirm is present. When soft=true failures are aggregated; when soft=false it behaves like a fail-fast assertion.
usages.contextHint: Use this for native browser alerts (JS alert/confirm), not for in-page toast/alert components (use AlertServiceFluent + AlertUiElement for those). Avoid separate usages for true/false—this flag toggles aggregation vs fail-fast.

items.summary: Switch WebDriver context to an iframe by its index.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToFrameByIndex(0)
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Enter an iframe when the application embeds content in frames and you need subsequent UI actions to operate inside that frame context.
usages.contextHint: Use index-based switching only when the frame order is stable. If the app changes frame ordering, prefer switching by name/id when available.

items.summary: Hard-assert that the current native browser alert text equals the expected value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .validateAlertText("Are you sure?")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Fail-fast validation of a native browser alert’s message (useful before accepting/dismissing).
usages.contextHint: Use this for native JS alerts/confirms. For in-page notifications/toasts, use AlertServiceFluent with AlertFields.* (AlertUiElement).

items.summary: Switch WebDriver context to the parent frame of the current iframe.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToParentFrame()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Move one level up in the frame hierarchy after completing actions inside a nested iframe.
usages.contextHint: Use when working with nested iframes. If you need to return to the top-level page directly, use switchToDefaultContent().

items.summary: Accept (OK) the currently displayed native browser alert/confirm.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .acceptAlert()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Confirm a native alert/confirm dialog (commonly used for destructive actions or confirmation prompts).
usages.contextHint: This handles native JS alerts/confirms. For closing in-page modals, use ModalServiceFluent with ModalFields.* (ModalUiElement).

items.summary: Close the current browser tab and switch to the next available tab (if present).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .closeCurrentTab()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Close a tab after opening an external link or a new tab flow, then return control to the remaining tab to continue the test.
usages.contextHint: Useful for flows that open new tabs/windows (downloads, help links, external pages). If you need deterministic targeting, pair with switchToWindow(title) or switchToNewTab().

items.summary: Navigate one step back in the browser history.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .back()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Return to the previous page/state using browser history (useful when validating navigation or returning from a details page).
usages.contextHint: Use back/forward for history-driven navigation. For app navigation via UI controls, prefer LinkServiceFluent/ButtonServiceFluent to reflect user behavior.

items.summary: Switch WebDriver context to a browser window whose title matches the provided value.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToWindow("Help")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Switch to a specific window by title after a new window opens (e.g., external auth, help/documentation window).
usages.contextHint: Use a distinctive window title to avoid ambiguous matches. If your app opens new tabs without reliable titles, switchToNewTab() may be simpler.

items.summary: Switch WebDriver context to an iframe by its name or id attribute.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToFrameByNameOrId("payment-frame")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Enter an iframe using a stable identifier when the application embeds content in frames and you need subsequent UI actions to operate inside that frame context.
usages.contextHint: Prefer name/id switching when available because it’s more stable than index-based switching. Return to the main page with switchToDefaultContent() when finished.

items.summary: Navigate one step forward in the browser history.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .forward()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Move forward after a back() call when validating navigation history behaviors.
usages.contextHint: Use back/forward for history-driven navigation. For app navigation via UI controls, prefer LinkServiceFluent/ButtonServiceFluent to reflect user behavior.

items.summary: Refresh the current browser page.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .refresh()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Reload the current page (useful to validate state persistence, recover from transient UI issues, or force re-rendering).
usages.contextHint: If refresh triggers loading, pair with loader waits where needed. Prefer app-level navigation when you want to mimic real user interactions.

items.summary: Open a new browser tab (via JavaScript) and switch focus to it.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .openNewTab()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Create a new tab when a test needs to run parallel navigation or isolate a new flow in a fresh tab.
usages.contextHint: Use with switchToWindow(...) or switchToNewTab() patterns when your flow opens multiple tabs. Closing the tab can be done with closeCurrentTab().

items.summary: Switch WebDriver context back to the default (top-level) document from within an iframe.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToDefaultContent()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Return to the main page context after completing interactions inside an iframe.
usages.contextHint: Use this when you need to fully exit frame context. If you only need to move up one frame level, use switchToParentFrame().

items.summary: Switch focus to a newly opened browser tab.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .switchToNewTab()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Switch to the most recently opened tab after a user action opens a new tab (e.g., clicking an external link).
usages.contextHint: Use when your flow opens a new tab and you want to continue in it. When finished, closeCurrentTab() can return focus to remaining tabs.

items.summary: Navigate the browser to the given URL (and apply the framework’s standard browser setup such as maximizing).

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .navigate("https://example.com")
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Open a page by URL as the start of a test or when a flow requires direct navigation rather than clicking through the UI.
usages.contextHint: Prefer using environment-configured base URLs/constants in real projects rather than hardcoding. After navigation, use component fluent services (input/button/link/etc.) for interactions.

items.summary: Dismiss (Cancel) the currently displayed native browser alert/confirm.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
  .browser()
  .dismissAlert()
  .drop()
  .complete();
usages.description: Cancel a native alert/confirm dialog (commonly used to abort an action when confirmation appears).
usages.contextHint: This handles native JS alerts/confirms. For closing in-page modals, use ModalServiceFluent with ModalFields.* (ModalUiElement).

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Modal behavior across different UI technologies/variants (open state checks, reading title/body content, clicking modal buttons, and closing). Concrete implementations implement this interface and are resolved at runtime via the ModalComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Modal defines the contract that ROA uses to interact with modal dialogs (asserting visibility/state, reading content, and performing actions like clicking buttons or closing). Technology-specific implementations provide the Selenium behavior and must implement all methods from this interface.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported modal technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding ModalComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Modal implementation at runtime.

items.summary: Retrieves the modal body text content.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations and assertions to verify modal message content (confirmations, warnings, descriptions). Implementations should reliably resolve the active modal and extract body content based on the UI technology’s markup.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure modal root resolution targets the currently active modal (handle stacked modals if supported) and normalize extracted text. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the modal content title (when distinct from the main header title).

usages.description: Used when the modal has a secondary title inside its content area (e.g., a section header within the body). Fluent validations can delegate here for precise assertions when both header and content titles exist.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: align the selector with your modal markup (content-title element) and consider fallback behavior when no separate content title exists. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks a modal button identified by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based button clicks are used when modal actions are best modeled by stable selectors (data-testid, ids, specific footer buttons). Fluent services delegate to this method when label matching is not desired or reliable.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: scope button resolution to the active modal root to avoid clicking similarly-named buttons behind the modal. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks a button identified by label within the provided container (typically the modal or a modal section).

usages.description: Container-scoped button clicks are used when multiple modals/sections exist and label matching must be scoped (e.g., click a footer button inside a specific modal instance). Fluent services use this to keep test interactions readable by expressing intent via button text.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep lookup relative to the container to avoid selecting a different matching button elsewhere. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Closes the currently active modal dialog.

usages.description: Used by fluent flows to dismiss a modal and return to the underlying page. Implementations should close via the modal’s intended mechanism (close button, dismiss control, ESC) and align with the technology’s behavior.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep close behavior consistent with the modal technology (close button/dismiss controls, ESC support) and handle animation/removal timing. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Clicks a modal button identified by label at the active modal scope.

usages.description: Label-based button clicks are commonly used for confirmation dialogs (OK/Cancel/Yes/No). Fluent services delegate to this method when the active modal is already the implied scope and the button text is stable.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: scope button resolution to the active modal root and normalize text matching for stability. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Checks whether a modal dialog is currently open/visible.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations and branching flows to assert modal presence before interacting with it or after closing it. Implementations should align open detection with the technology (show class, aria-modal, overlay presence).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement open detection using stable signals for your modal technology (visible state vs present in DOM) and consider animation timing. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Retrieves the modal header title text.

usages.description: Used by fluent validations to assert dialog titles (e.g., “Confirm”, “Delete item”, “Details”). Implementations should reliably resolve the active modal and extract the header title according to the modal technology’s markup.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure title extraction targets the correct header element for your modal technology and normalize whitespace. Test-side: prefer ModalServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).modal() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Test-side postcondition/cleanup annotation. Declares one or more cleanup targets that the framework executes after the test finishes (teardown) to remove created data and keep environments clean. Targets are typically written as constants from a project enum implementing DataRipper (e.g., DataCleaner implements DataRipper<DataCleaner>).

usages.description: Single cleanup target: declare one teardown action to run automatically after the test. In real projects, the target is typically written as a constant from an enum implementing DataRipper (e.g., DataCleaner implements DataRipper<DataCleaner>), so cleanup actions are centrally registered and reusable across tests.
usages.contextHint: Use on @Test methods. Prefer declarative cleanup via @Ripper targets instead of embedding teardown logic inside the test body. Targets should reference your project cleanup registry (DataRipper enum constants/keys).

usages.description: Multiple cleanup targets: declare more than one teardown action when a test creates multiple data types that must be removed after execution. This keeps teardown declarative even for complex scenarios.
usages.contextHint: Use multiple targets when the test leaves behind multiple entities/resources. In real projects, targets typically come from a DataRipper enum (e.g., DataCleaner implements DataRipper<DataCleaner>) so cleanup stays consistent and centrally managed.

items.summary: Framework-side component contract for Link behavior across different UI technologies/variants. Link extends Button, so implementations must provide all Button operations (click/state checks, etc.) plus link-specific actions such as doubleClick. Implementations are resolved at runtime via the LinkComponentType identifier matched through @ImplementationOfType.

usages.description: Link builds on the Button contract: it inherits all button-like behaviors and adds link-specific interactions (e.g., double-click). Technology-specific implementations must implement the full inherited Button API as well as the Link methods, so ROA can treat links consistently across technologies.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: implement this interface once per supported link technology/variant and annotate the class with @ImplementationOfType using the same identifier as the corresponding LinkComponentType. ROA uses that mapping to resolve the correct Link implementation at runtime. Because Link extends Button, ensure your implementation overrides the full Button method set in addition to Link-specific methods so runtime delegation is complete and consistent.

items.summary: Performs a double-click on the link identified by locator.

usages.description: Locator-based double-click is used when a link is best modeled by a stable selector (id, data-testid, etc.). Fluent services delegate to this method in advanced cases where text matching is not appropriate.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: ensure the locator targets the intended clickable link element and consider stability (scroll into view, wait clickable) before performing the action. Test-side: prefer LinkServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).link() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Performs a double-click on the first link-like element resolved within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped double-click is used when the flow already narrowed scope to a specific region and expects a primary link inside it (row/cell/card). Fluent services can use this when container scoping is required to avoid matching a different link elsewhere.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep resolution relative to the container and align link selectors with your technology (a tags, role='link', link-styled buttons). Test-side: prefer LinkServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).link() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Performs a double-click on a link identified by its visible text at the page (global) scope.

usages.description: Global text-based double-click is used when link text uniquely identifies the link on the page. Fluent services may prefer container-scoped overloads on complex pages where the same text can appear in multiple regions.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: normalize text matching and keep global selection only when safe to avoid double-clicking the wrong link. Test-side: prefer LinkServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).link() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Performs a double-click on a link identified by its visible text within the provided container.

usages.description: Container-scoped text double-click is used when multiple identical link texts exist on the page and the intended one must be resolved relative to a specific region (table row, card, modal). Fluent services delegate to this to keep interactions readable and correctly scoped.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: keep the lookup relative to the container (use ".//" selectors) so resolution stays within scope. Test-side: prefer LinkServiceFluent via quest.use(RING_OF_UI).link() rather than instantiating or calling component implementations directly.

items.summary: Project-side contract for supplying static key-value test data that the framework loads into quest storage before the test runs. Used together with @StaticTestData(Provider.class).

usages.description: Implement StaticDataProvider in your test project to define a map of reusable values (credentials, fixed form inputs, known UI configuration values) that can be preloaded before a test via @StaticTestData.
usages.contextHint: Project-side provider class. The framework instantiates it and loads its staticTestData() map into storage when @StaticTestData(UiStaticData.class) is present. Tests typically read values via DataExtractorsTest.staticTestData(key) + retrieve(...).

items.summary: Marks a test method as part of the Smoke suite (wraps JUnit @Tag("Smoke")).

usages.description: Tag a single UI test method as Smoke (fast, high-signal checks like critical paths).
usages.contextHint: Use on @Test methods only. Prefer Smoke for a small set of tests that must run quickly and catch major breakages.

items.summary: Framework-side default Table implementation used when a project does not provide a specialized table implementation. It handles standard table behaviors (row lookup/mapping) for the common/default table type.

usages.description: DefaultTable is the baseline table behavior the framework falls back to for DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT (or when no custom TableComponentType implementation is required).
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: prefer the default implementation for standard HTML/Bootstrap-like tables. Only introduce a dedicated @ImplementationOfType-bound Table implementation when your table widget requires specialized behavior beyond what DefaultTable supports.

items.summary: Core fluent "UI ring" service that wires and exposes all UI component fluent services (input/button/radio/checkbox/select/list/loader/link/alert/tab/modal/accordion/table/insertion/interceptor/navigation/validation) on top of a SmartWebDriver + Quest storage. In real projects you typically extend it with an application facade (e.g., AppUiService extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService>) to provide a typed, ergonomic entrypoint returned by quest.use(RING_OF_UI).

usages.description: Use UiServiceFluent as the base UI ring facade, but expose it in your project through an application-specific subclass (AppUiService). The subclass binds the self-type (for fluent chaining), receives SmartWebDriver + SuperQuest from the framework, calls postQuestSetupInitialization() to wire all component fluent services, and then provides ergonomic shorthand methods (input/button/browser/etc.) that return the underlying fluent services.
usages.contextHint: Framework-side: UiServiceFluent is constructed/provided by the UI test extension and wired against Quest storage; do not instantiate fluent services manually in tests. Project-side: create a single facade (e.g., AppUiService) that extends UiServiceFluent<AppUiService>, sets `this.quest`, and calls `postQuestSetupInitialization()` in the constructor. Test-side: obtain it via `quest.use(RING_OF_UI)` and call component services (`.browser()`, `.button()`, `.input()`, etc.); end chains with `.drop()` to return to Quest and `.complete()` to finalize assertions.

items.summary: Run an inline validation block inside the active UI fluent chain. Use this overload when you just want to execute a few assertions or checks (no arguments) while staying in the same UiServiceFluent flow before continuing with other UI operations.

usages.description: Inject a small custom validation block into an existing UI chain. This is useful when the framework already navigated/performed actions and you want to assert some additional conditions that don’t justify a dedicated fluent method.
usages.contextHint: Keep Runnable validations lightweight and deterministic. If your validations need access to SoftAssertions or the current driver, use the Consumer overload instead. Prefer built-in fluent validations (validateIsVisible/validateText/etc.) for standard checks and reserve validate(Runnable) for one-off project assertions.

items.summary: Run a grouped validation block using a SoftAssertions-style consumer within the active UI fluent chain. Use this overload to perform multiple related checks in one place while letting the assertion collector report all failures together.

usages.description: Inject a custom validation step that receives a SoftAssertions collector, allowing multiple checks to be evaluated and reported together without stopping at the first failure. This is ideal for verifying a whole UI state snapshot (e.g., several fields/messages) after one action.
usages.contextHint: Use this overload when you want aggregated assertions (SoftAssertions). Keep checks focused on UI state and prefer enum-backed UiElement registries for element targeting. If you only need a simple no-arg validation block, use validate(Runnable). Standard element validations (validateText/validateIsVisible/etc.) should remain the default choice.

items.summary: Framework-provided TableComponentType enum that represents the default table technology/variant used by the UI table service. DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT maps to the DefaultTable implementation and is used whenever a TableElement does not provide a specific TableComponentType.

usages.description: Use DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT to explicitly declare that a table should use the framework’s default table behavior. In most cases you can omit the table type from your TableElement enum entirely—TableElement.super.tableType() defaults to DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT, so the DefaultTable implementation is selected automatically.
usages.contextHint: Project-side: specify a TableComponentType only when you need a non-default table behavior; otherwise omit it and rely on the default mapping. Framework-side: DefaultTableTypes.DEFAULT resolves to DefaultTable, providing standard table read/mapping behavior.

items.summary: Selection strategy enum (RANDOM, FIRST, LAST, ALL) used by UI components/services to choose element(s) when a specific target is not predetermined. Strategy also implements ComponentType so it can be passed consistently into component APIs that support strategy-based selection.

usages.description: Use Strategy when the test or flow should pick an element dynamically (first, last, random, or all) rather than by explicit label/value. This is commonly used by component methods like expand/collapse/select that accept Strategy.
usages.contextHint: Use Strategy.FIRST / Strategy.LAST / Strategy.RANDOM / Strategy.ALL directly (no helper methods). Prefer Strategy-based methods for exploratory flows or when labels are not stable/known up-front.

items.summary: Enum of table assertion modes used by ROA table validations (e.g., contains row, does not contain, row count checks, column/value expectations). Table assertion helpers use these types to express what should be validated without hardcoding assertion logic in tests.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
     .table()
     .validate(
         Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS,
         TableAssertionTypes.CONTAINS_ROW,
         "TELECOM"
     )
     .drop().complete();
usages.description: Use TableAssertionTypes when invoking table validation helpers to declare the kind of check you want to perform (the table service interprets the type and executes the corresponding assertion logic).
usages.contextHint: Prefer table fluent validations that accept TableAssertionTypes rather than implementing ad-hoc assertions over raw row strings. Choose the assertion type that matches the intent (presence/absence/count/value checks) and keep indicators/criteria specific.

items.summary: Core assertion type enum for generic comparison semantics (used when validations are not domain-specific), commonly paired with generic AssertionTarget enums in validator flows.

usages.code:
Assertion a = Assertion.builder()
    .target(UiTablesAssertionTarget.TABLE_VALUES)
    .type(AssertionTypes.CONTAINS)
    .expected("TELECOM")
    .soft(true)
    .build();

quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
    .table().validate(Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS, a)
    .drop().complete();
usages.description: Use AssertionTypes when you want generic comparison semantics (e.g., contains/equals-like checks) applied through the validation layer.
usages.contextHint: Prefer domain-specific assertion enums (e.g., TableAssertionTypes) when available for clearer intent. Use AssertionTypes for generic checks where the validator target exposes a simple comparable value.

items.summary: Enum describing what part of a UI table a validation targets (e.g., table-level, row-level, cell-level or header-level targets depending on the assertion). Used by table validation helpers to route an assertion to the correct table artifact.

usages.code:
quest.use(RING_OF_UI)
     .table()
     .validate(
         Tables.ALL_TRANSACTIONS,
         UiTablesAssertionTarget.ROW,
         TableAssertionTypes.CONTAINS_ROW,
         "TELECOM"
     )
     .drop().complete();
usages.description: Use UiTablesAssertionTarget to specify the focus of a table assertion (what the assertion is evaluating). The table validation layer uses the target together with TableAssertionTypes to execute the right kind of check.
usages.contextHint: Prefer table fluent validations that accept UiTablesAssertionTarget and TableAssertionTypes so assertions stay consistent and readable. Choose the target that matches your intent (e.g., validate against rows vs specific cells/headers).
